;; CORRECT: use syntax-case with lambda wrapper
(define-syntax my-macro
  (lambda (stx)
    (syntax-case stx ()
      ((_ x)
       #'(my-helper x)))))

;; Multi-clause with rest args:
(define-syntax with-thing
  (lambda (stx)
    (syntax-case stx ()
      ((_ obj expr)
       #'(let ((o obj))
           (thing-acquire! o)
           (let ((result (guard (e (else (thing-release! o) (raise e)))
                           expr)))
             (thing-release! o)
             result)))
      ((_ obj expr rest ...)
       #'(with-thing obj (begin expr rest ...))))))
notes: In Jerboa/Chez R6RS libraries, syntax-rules macros fail with 'misplaced aux keyword' errors when the template references library-defined procedures (like spin-lock!, my-helper). This is because syntax-rules templates are opaque — they can't capture lexical bindings from the library scope. Always use (define-syntax name (lambda (stx) (syntax-case stx () ...))) with #' templates instead. The #' (syntax) form properly captures the lexical environment.

title: Custom record constructor to avoid name conflicts
code:
;; Problem: define-record-type auto-generates make-foo, but you want
;; a custom constructor (e.g., with default arguments via case-lambda).
;; Using (define make-foo ...) after define-record-type causes
;; "multiple definitions" error.

;; Solution: use 3-name form to give internal constructor a different name
(define-record-type (%foo %make-foo foo?)
  (nongenerative foo-unique-id)
  (sealed #t)
  (fields (immutable x) (immutable y)))

;; Now define public constructor with defaults
(define make-foo
  (case-lambda
    (()    (%make-foo 0 0))
    ((x)   (%make-foo x 0))
    ((x y) (%make-foo x y))))

;; Accessors use the first name: %foo-x, %foo-y
;; Predicate uses third name: foo?
notes: The 3-name form is (define-record-type (internal-name constructor-name predicate-name)). Accessors are prefixed with internal-name (e.g., %foo-x). If you want public accessors with a nice name, define wrapper procedures or use a different naming convention like $foo for internal.

title: Efficient list building with with-list-builder
code:
;; Macro form: bind a collector function
(with-list-builder (c)
  (c 1) (c 2) (c 3))  ; => (1 2 3)

;; Procedural form: pass a procedure that receives the collector
(call-with-list-builder
  (lambda (c)
    (do ((i 0 (+ i 1)))
      ((= i 5))
      (c (* i i)))))  ; => (0 1 4 9 16)

;; Useful for building lists in imperative loops or callbacks
;; where reverse-accumulate pattern is awkward:
(with-list-builder (add!)
  (for-each (lambda (x)
              (when (even? x)
                (add! (* x 10))))
    '(1 2 3 4 5 6)))  ; => (20 40 60)
notes: Ported from Gerbil v0.19's with-list-builder. Uses a mutable tail pointer for O(1) append. The collector function (c) appends to the end of the list, maintaining insertion order without needing reverse at the end. Much more efficient than (reverse (fold ...)) for large lists.

title: Resolving import conflicts in (jerboa prelude)
code:
;; When building a prelude that re-exports from multiple modules,
;; use (only ...) instead of (except ...) to avoid errors when
;; the excluded symbol doesn't exist in the source module.

;; WRONG: errors if sugar doesn't export 'while'
;; (except (std sugar) try catch finally while)

;; CORRECT: explicitly list what you want
;; (only (std sugar)
;;   assert! chain chain-and
;;   unwind-protect with-id with-lock with-catch
;;   cut cute <> <...> ...)

;; For Chez builtins that clash with custom modules:
;; (except (chezscheme)
;;   make-hash-table hash-table?   ; clashes with (jerboa runtime)
;;   sort sort!                     ; clashes with (std sort)
;;   partition                      ; clashes with (std misc list)
;;   printf fprintf                 ; clashes with (std format)
;;   iota 1+ 1-)                   ; clashes with (jerboa runtime)
notes: R6RS (except ...) is strict: it errors if the excluded symbol is not actually exported by the source library. This means removing an export from a module can break downstream except clauses. Prefer (only ...) for fragile multi-module prelude assembly. Common Chez/Jerboa clashes: partition, sort, sort!, printf, fprintf, make-hash-table, hash-table?, iota, 1+, 1-, path-extension, path-absolute?.

title: Typed scope with dot-access via using
code:
;; Define a struct
(defstruct point (x y))

;; Checked: runtime type check + dot-access
(using (p (make-point 3 4) : point)
  (+ p.x p.y))  ;; => 7, p.x expands to (point-x p)

;; Unchecked: dot-access only, no runtime check
(using (p (make-point 3 4) as point)
  p.x)  ;; => 3

;; Multiple bindings
(defstruct rect (w h))
(using ((p (make-point 1 2) : point)
        (r (make-rect 10 20) : rect))
  (+ p.x r.w))  ;; => 11
notes: v1 supports single-level dot access only (p.x works, p.a.b doesn't). The : variant checks the type in debug mode (*typed-mode*); the as variant skips the check. Quoted symbols like 'p.x are NOT transformed. Multi-binding expands to nested single-binding using calls.

title: Define functions with inline type annotations
code:
;; Typed args: (param : type)
(def (add (x : fixnum) (y : fixnum))
  (fx+ x y))

;; With return type
(def (greet (name : string)) : string
  (string-append "hello " name))

;; Mixed typed and untyped params
(def (lookup (key : string) default)
  (hashtable-ref some-table key default))

;; Type checks respect *typed-mode*:
;; 'debug (default) = checks enabled, raises on mismatch
;; 'release = checks stripped, zero overhead
notes: Uses (param : type) syntax in def formals. The : must be a symbol (space-separated). Works alongside optional args — (param default) is 2-element, (param : type) is 3-element, no ambiguity. Requires (std typed) to be imported (for check-type! and check-return-type!). Types use the same registry as define/t: fixnum, string, boolean, pair, list, number, symbol, etc. plus custom types via register-type-predicate!.

title: Inline checked type cast with :
code:
;; (: expr type) — checked cast, raises in debug mode
(: 42 fixnum)        ;; => 42
(: "hello" string)   ;; => "hello"
;; (: "oops" fixnum) ;; => ERROR in debug mode

;; Contract predicates
((maybe string?) #f)     ;; => #t (#f always passes)
((maybe string?) "hi")   ;; => #t
((list-of? fixnum?) '(1 2 3))  ;; => #t
notes: The : operator cannot be named :- or :~ because Jerboa's reader treats : as keyword prefix (so :- becomes keyword -). Use (: expr type) for checked casts. maybe and list-of? return predicates, not values — use them with guard, assert, or define/contract.

title: Jerboa reader colon-prefix keyword gotcha
code:
;; The Jerboa reader treats : as a keyword prefix.
;; :- reads as keyword -, not symbol :-
;; :~ reads as keyword ~, not symbol :~
;; :? reads as keyword ?, not symbol :?

;; Verify:
;; ':-  => (-)     ;; NOT the symbol :-
;; ':~  => (~)     ;; NOT the symbol :~
;; ':   => :       ;; colon alone IS a valid symbol
;; '::  => garbled ;; double colon gets mangled

;; SAFE symbols for define-syntax names:
;; : (bare colon with space after) — works fine
;; as, is, of — plain words, always safe

;; UNSAFE — cannot be used as macro/export names:
;; :- :~ :? :> :: :foo  — all become keyword forms

;; Workaround: use |:-| pipe-escaping for identifiers
'|:-|  ;; => the symbol :-
;; But this is ugly — prefer renaming to : or as
notes: This matters when porting Gerbil's :- and :~ type operators to Jerboa. You cannot define-syntax a name starting with : (except bare :) because the reader converts it to keyword syntax before the expander sees it. The (std ergo) module uses : for checked cast and as for unchecked annotation as a workaround.

title: Walk and transform syntax tree in macros
code:
;; Pattern: walk a syntax tree and replace symbols matching a condition.
;; Used by with-fixnum-ops (std typed) and using (std ergo).

(define-syntax my-transform
  (lambda (stx)
    ;; Walk s-expression, replacing via rewrite-map
    (define (transform s rewrite-map ctx)
      (let ([d (syntax->datum s)])
        (cond
          ;; Symbol matches rewrite map
          [(and (symbol? d) (assq d rewrite-map))
           => (lambda (entry)
                (datum->syntax ctx (cdr entry)))]
          ;; Not a pair — return as-is
          [(not (pair? d)) s]
          ;; Don't descend into quotes
          [(eq? (car d) 'quote) s]
          ;; List — recurse into each element
          [else
           (let ([lst (syntax->list s)])
             (if lst
               (datum->syntax ctx
                 (map (lambda (x)
                        (syntax->datum (transform x rewrite-map ctx)))
                      lst))
               s))])))

    (syntax-case stx ()
      [(_ body ...)
       (let ([rmap '((old-name . new-name))])
         (with-syntax ([(tb ...)
                        (map (lambda (b)
                               (datum->syntax #'stx
                                 (syntax->datum (transform b rmap #'stx))))
                             (syntax->list #'(body ...)))])
           #'(begin tb ...)))])))
notes: Key insight: use (syntax->datum s) to inspect, (datum->syntax ctx ...) to rebuild with proper lexical context. The ctx argument should be a syntax object from the macro use site so that generated identifiers resolve in the user's scope. Always skip (quote ...) forms to avoid transforming quoted data. This pattern is O(n) in the size of the syntax tree — fine for typical macro bodies.

title: QScintilla: Re-apply syntax lexer when buffer appears in a new editor widget
code:
;; QScintilla lexers (and keyword lists) are per-widget, NOT per-document.
;; When sharing a document across multiple editor widgets (e.g. split windows),
;; each new widget needs the lexer explicitly re-applied.
;;
;; The shared Scintilla document (SCI_SETDOCPOINTER) preserves text and undo
;; history, but the lexer state lives in the widget. After attaching a buffer
;; to a new editor, check if the buffer has a stored lexer-lang and re-apply it:

(def (reapply-lexer-to-widget! ed buf)
  (let ((lang (buffer-lexer-lang buf)))
    (when lang
      (let ((lexer-name (language->lexer-name lang)))
        (when lexer-name
          ;; Re-set the lexer on this specific widget
          (qt-scintilla-set-lexer-language! ed lexer-name)
          ;; Re-apply keywords (QsciLexer::setLexer resets them)
          (case lang
            ((shell) (sci-send/string ed SCI_SETKEYWORDS *shell-keywords* 0))
            ;; ... other languages ...
            (else (void)))
          ;; Restore margin/background colors (setLexer resets these too)
          (restore-margin-colors! ed)
          (qt-enable-code-folding! ed))))))

;; Call this from post-buffer-attach-hook or after SCI_SETDOCPOINTER:
;;   (add-hook! 'post-buffer-attach-hook
;;     (lambda (editor buf)
;;       (reapply-lexer-to-widget! editor buf)))
notes: This pattern applies whenever SCI_SETDOCPOINTER is used to share a document across editor widgets (split windows, multi-frame setups). The lexer, keywords (SCI_SETKEYWORDS), and margin colors are all per-widget and must be re-applied to each new widget. Also note: org-mode uses manual styling (SCI_SETILEXER=4033 to disable lexer) so handle it separately. SCI_DELETEBACK (2326) works correctly for multibyte characters and is simpler than manual SCI_GETCURRENTPOS + SCI_DELETERANGE math.

title: QScintilla: Buffer type predicates match by lexer-lang, not by runtime state
code:
;; PITFALL: shell-buffer? (and similar predicates) return #t based on lexer-lang,
;; NOT based on whether an interactive shell session is active.
;;
;; Example: opening a shell script file ~/foo.sh gives it lexer-lang=shell,
;; so shell-buffer? returns #t — even though there's no shell state.
;;
;; (def (shell-buffer? buf)
;;   (eq? (buffer-lexer-lang buf) 'shell))   ;; <-- matches ALL shell-lang buffers!
;;
;; Commands that branch on shell-buffer? must also check for active shell state.
;; Without this check, editing commands silently do nothing on shell script files.

;; WRONG — silently fails on shell script files (no shell-state entry):
(cond
  ((shell-buffer? buf)
   (let ((ss (hash-get *shell-state* buf)))
     (cond
       ((and ss (shell-pty-busy? ss)) ...)   ;; only runs if interactive shell
       ((and ss (> pos (shell-state-prompt-pos ss))) ...)))))  ;; same

;; CORRECT — add fallthrough for file buffers without shell state:
(cond
  ((shell-buffer? buf)
   (let ((ss (hash-get *shell-state* buf)))
     (cond
       ((and ss (shell-pty-busy? ss))
        (shell-send-input! ss (string (integer->char 127))))
       ((and ss (> pos (shell-state-prompt-pos ss)))
        (qt-backward-delete-char! ed))
       ((not ss)
        ;; No shell state = regular shell script file being edited
        (qt-backward-delete-char! ed))))))
notes: This pattern generalizes to any command that dispatches on buffer type predicates backed by lexer-lang. Always check for active runtime state (shell-state, terminal-state, repl-state) separately from the type predicate. The symptom is silent failure: the command runs (no error) but does nothing, because none of the cond branches match. Debug by checking (hash-get *shell-state* buf) via IPC REPL.

title: Capture stdout to string via parameterize + open-output-string
code:
;; Capture stdout from a thunk into a string, letting stderr pass through
(define (call-with-captured-stdout thunk)
  (let ([out-port (open-output-string)])
    (parameterize ([current-output-port out-port])
      (thunk))
    (get-output-string out-port)))

;; Usage:
(let ([result (call-with-captured-stdout
                (lambda ()
                  (display "hello\n")
                  (printf "count: ~a~n" 42)))])
  (printf "captured: ~s~n" result))
;; => captured: "hello\ncount: 42\n"
notes: Use open-output-string + get-output-string, NOT call-with-string-output-port, when you need to read the accumulated output after an error (e.g. inside guard). call-with-string-output-port won't return the port's contents if the thunk raises. For error capture, combine with guard and get-output-string in the guard clause.

title: Intercept (exit n) in a subcommand without terminating the host process
code:
;; When embedding a CLI tool (e.g. pssm-main) inside a shell/REPL,
;; calling (exit n) would kill the host. Override exit-handler to
;; raise a tagged vector instead, then catch it with guard.

(define my-exit-tag (vector 'my-exit))

(define (run-subcommand thunk)
  (let ([out-port (open-output-string)])
    (guard (e [#t
               (cond
                 ;; Caught our exit-handler override
                 [(and (vector? e)
                       (= (vector-length e) 2)
                       (eq? (vector-ref e 0) my-exit-tag))
                  (values (get-output-string out-port)
                          (vector-ref e 1))]  ; exit code
                 ;; Any other error
                 [else
                  (values (string-append
                            (get-output-string out-port)
                            (call-with-string-output-port
                              (lambda (p) (display-condition e p))))
                          1)])])
      (parameterize ([current-output-port out-port]
                     [exit-handler
                      (lambda args
                        (raise (vector my-exit-tag
                                 (if (pair? args) (car args) 0))))])
        (thunk)
        (values (get-output-string out-port) 0)))))

;; Usage:
(let-values ([(output code) (run-subcommand
                              (lambda () (display "done\n") (exit 0)))])
  (printf "output=~s code=~a~n" output code))
;; => output="done\n" code=0
notes: The exit-handler parameter is Chez-specific. The tag must be a fresh vector (use eq? identity), not a symbol, to avoid false matches. Always use guard (not with-exception-handler) so the continuation is abandoned. Combine with current-output-port parameterize to capture stdout while letting stderr flow to terminal.

title: Tokenize shell-style arguments with single/double quotes and backslash escapes
code:
;; Shell-style argument tokenizer: splits a string into tokens
;; respecting single quotes, double quotes, and backslash escapes.
;; "pssm web-* 'df -h'" => ("pssm" "web-*" "df -h")

(define (tokenize-args str)
  (let ([len (string-length str)])
    (let loop ([i 0] [tokens '()] [current '()]
               [in-sq #f] [in-dq #f] [escaped #f])
      (cond
        [(= i len)
         (let ([tok (list->string (reverse current))])
           (reverse (if (> (string-length tok) 0)
                      (cons tok tokens)
                      tokens)))]
        [escaped
         (loop (+ i 1) tokens (cons (string-ref str i) current)
               in-sq in-dq #f)]
        [(and (not in-sq) (not in-dq) (char=? (string-ref str i) #\\))
         (loop (+ i 1) tokens current in-sq in-dq #t)]
        [(and (not in-dq) (char=? (string-ref str i) #\'))
         (loop (+ i 1) tokens current (not in-sq) in-dq #f)]
        [(and (not in-sq) (char=? (string-ref str i) #\"))
         (loop (+ i 1) tokens current in-sq (not in-dq) #f)]
        [(and (not in-sq) (not in-dq)
              (char-whitespace? (string-ref str i)))
         (let ([tok (list->string (reverse current))])
           (loop (+ i 1)
                 (if (> (string-length tok) 0)
                   (cons tok tokens) tokens)
                 '() #f #f #f))]
        [else
         (loop (+ i 1) tokens (cons (string-ref str i) current)
               in-sq in-dq #f)]))))

;; Examples:
(tokenize-args "ls -la /tmp")        ;=> ("ls" "-la" "/tmp")
(tokenize-args "echo 'hello world'") ;=> ("echo" "hello world")
(tokenize-args "cmd \"a b\" c")      ;=> ("cmd" "a b" "c")
(tokenize-args "path\\ name")        ;=> ("path name")
notes: Pure Scheme, no dependencies. Uses tail-recursive loop with character-by-character state machine. Does NOT handle nested quotes or $variable expansion — this is intentional for simple meta-command parsing. For full POSIX shell parsing, use an external tool.

title: Write string to file (with overwrite support)
code:
;; Write to a NEW file (errors if file exists):
(call-with-output-file "output.txt"
  (lambda (port)
    (display "hello world" port)
    (newline port)))

;; OVERWRITE an existing file (use 'replace mode):
(let ((port (open-output-file "output.txt" 'replace)))
  (display "hello world" port)
  (close-port port))

;; GOTCHA: In Chez Scheme (R6RS), call-with-output-file raises
;; "file already exists" if the file exists. You MUST use
;; open-output-file with 'replace to overwrite.
notes: call-with-output-file is R6RS-strict and fails on existing files. For save-buffer style operations, always use open-output-file with 'replace. The 'replace option is Chez-specific (not R6RS standard). Other options: 'truncate (same effect), 'append, 'error (default).

title: Pump Qt event queue during long operations for UI responsiveness
code:
;; When a long-running operation blocks the Qt event loop (e.g. terminal
;; rendering with many FFI calls), the UI becomes unresponsive — M-x,
;; keyboard shortcuts, etc. stop working.
;;
;; Fix: call qt-app-process-events! periodically to pump pending events.

;; Store a global reference to the Qt application:
(def *qt-app-ref* #f)
;; Set it during init:
(set! *qt-app-ref* qt-app)

;; Then in your long-running loop or after heavy processing:
(when *qt-app-ref*
  (qt-app-process-events! *qt-app-ref*))

;; Real example: vterm rendering after vtscreen-feed! (3840+ FFI calls
;; for row-diff + per-cell colors). Without process-events, keystrokes
;; queue up and M-x never fires during flooding commands like `find ~/ -ls`.

;; Also useful in minibuffer polling loops:
;; (let loop ()
;;   (qt-app-process-events! app)
;;   (when (not done?) (loop)))
notes: qt-app-process-events! dispatches all pending events (key presses, mouse, paint, timers). Call it between chunks of work, not inside tight inner loops (each call has FFI overhead). Combine with a byte/time budget to cap how much work happens per tick before pumping events.

title: Cap PTY data per timer tick with a byte budget for responsiveness
code:
;; When a PTY reader thread posts data chunks to a channel, the main-thread
;; timer tick drains all available chunks. For flooding commands (find ~/ -ls),
;; this can accumulate megabytes per tick, blocking the event loop.
;;
;; Fix: set a byte budget per tick. Process up to the budget, then stop
;; and let the next tick handle the rest.

(def *pty-batch-budget* 65536)  ;; 64KB per tick

(let drain ((chunks []) (bytes 0) (done-msg #f))
  (if (and (> bytes 0) (>= bytes *pty-batch-budget*))
    ;; Budget exhausted — render what we have, leave rest for next tick
    (let ((combined (apply string-append (reverse chunks))))
      (process-batch! combined))
    ;; Try to get more data (non-blocking)
    (let ((msg (channel-try-get ch)))
      (cond
        ((not msg)
         ;; Channel empty — render accumulated chunks
         (unless (null? chunks)
           (let ((combined (apply string-append (reverse chunks))))
             (process-batch! combined))))
        ((string? msg)
         (drain (cons msg chunks)
                (+ bytes (string-length msg))
                done-msg))
        (else
         ;; Control message (e.g. process-done sentinel)
         (drain chunks bytes msg))))))
notes: The byte budget alone may not be sufficient for UI responsiveness if the rendering step itself is expensive (e.g. vtscreen row-diff with per-cell FFI color calls). Combine with qt-app-process-events! (see qt-process-events-responsiveness recipe) to pump the event queue between render batches.

title: Capture stdout+stderr at fd level using dup2 and fresh Scheme ports
code:
;; Capture stdout (fd 1) and stderr (fd 2) to a file at the OS level.
;; Subprocesses (fork/exec) inherit the redirected fds and CANNOT escape.
;; Uses fresh Scheme ports to avoid position-tracking conflicts with the
;; old terminal-backed ports.
;;
;; Requires: ffi-dup, ffi-dup2, ffi-close-fd, ffi-open-raw from (jsh ffi)
;; O_WRONLY=1, O_CREAT=64, O_TRUNC=512 → bitwise-ior = 577

(let* ((file-fd (ffi-open-raw "/tmp/captured.txt" 577 438))  ;; O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, mode 0666
       (saved-out-port (current-output-port))
       (saved-err-port (current-error-port)))
  ;; Flush existing ports before redirecting their underlying fd
  (flush-output-port saved-out-port)
  (flush-output-port saved-err-port)
  ;; Save original fds
  (let ((saved-1 (ffi-dup 1))
        (saved-2 (ffi-dup 2)))
    ;; Redirect fd 1 and fd 2 to the file
    (ffi-dup2 file-fd 1)
    (ffi-dup2 file-fd 2)
    (ffi-close-fd file-fd)
    ;; Create FRESH Scheme ports on dup'd copies of the redirected fds.
    ;; Critical: do NOT reuse the old ports — their internal position
    ;; tracking was for a terminal (non-seekable) and will corrupt file
    ;; writes when executor.sls calls output-port-byte-position to sync.
    (let* ((cap-port (transcoded-port
                       (open-fd-output-port (ffi-dup 1) (buffer-mode line))
                       (make-transcoder (utf-8-codec))))
           (err-port (transcoded-port
                       (open-fd-output-port (ffi-dup 2) (buffer-mode line))
                       (make-transcoder (utf-8-codec)))))
      (current-output-port cap-port)
      (current-error-port err-port)
      ;; === Run your command here ===
      ;; (run-cmd cmd-str)  ;; or any code that writes to stdout/stderr
      ;; === Cleanup ===
      (flush-output-port cap-port)
      (flush-output-port err-port)
      (close-port cap-port)
      (close-port err-port)
      ;; Restore original fds and ports
      (ffi-dup2 saved-1 1)
      (ffi-dup2 saved-2 2)
      (ffi-close-fd saved-1)
      (ffi-close-fd saved-2)
      (current-output-port saved-out-port)
      (current-error-port saved-err-port))))
notes:
GOTCHA 1: Do NOT use (open-output-file "/dev/fd/1") after dup2 — Chez raises 'file exists' because its port tracker sees a conflict with /dev/fd/1.

GOTCHA 2: Do NOT reuse the old (current-output-port) after dup2'ing fd 1 to a file. The old port was created for a terminal (non-seekable). When executor.sls calls (output-port-byte-position port end-pos) after waitpid to sync Scheme's position with fd-level writes, the stale port position overwrites earlier data in the file. Fresh ports via open-fd-output-port start at position 0 and stay in sync.

GOTCHA 3: Use (ffi-dup 1) for the port fd, not fd 1 directly. close-port closes its fd — using a dup prevents accidentally closing fd 1 itself.

GOTCHA 4: O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC = 577 (decimal). Mode 438 = 0666 octal. These are Linux x86_64 values.

title: Force font family+size on all QScintilla styles after setting a lexer
code:
;; PITFALL: QsciLexer::setLexer() resets the font on all 128 styles
;; to the lexer's default. If the user set a custom font size (e.g. 20pt),
;; the lexer overrides it back to the default (often 10pt or 12pt).
;;
;; Fix: After any call to qt-scintilla-set-lexer-language!, re-force
;; the font family and size on all 128 styles:

(let loop ((i 0))
  (when (<= i 127)
    (sci-send/string ed SCI_STYLESETFONT *default-font-family* i)
    (sci-send ed SCI_STYLESETSIZE i *default-font-size*)
    (loop (+ i 1))))

;; Also restore margin colors (the font loop may corrupt line-number style):
(let ((ln-face (face-get 'line-number)))
  (when (and ln-face (face-bg ln-face))
    (let-values (((r g b) (parse-hex-color (face-bg ln-face))))
      (sci-send ed SCI_STYLESETBACK STYLE_LINENUMBER (rgb->sci r g b))
      (sci-send ed 2260 0 (rgb->sci r g b))))  ;; 2260 = SCI_SETFOLDMARGINCOLOUR
  (when (and ln-face (face-fg ln-face))
    (let-values (((r g b) (parse-hex-color (face-fg ln-face))))
      (sci-send ed SCI_STYLESETFORE STYLE_LINENUMBER (rgb->sci r g b)))))
notes: This applies to split-window (qt-make-new-window!), buffer switching (post-buffer-attach-hook), and any path that calls qt-scintilla-set-lexer-language!. The 128 styles (0-127) cover all Scintilla style IDs including STYLE_DEFAULT(32) and STYLE_LINENUMBER(33). See also: apply-font-to-all-editors! in commands-config.ss for the global set-font-size command.

title: Activate tree-sitter as primary syntax highlighter (replacing Scintilla lexer)
code:
;; To use tree-sitter instead of Scintilla's Lexilla lexer:
;; 1. Disable the built-in lexer (SCI_SETILEXER = 4033)
(sci-send ed 4033 0)

;; 2. Initialize tree-sitter parser + compile highlight query
(when (not (ts-buffer-state buf))
  (ts-buffer-init! buf ts-name))  ;; ts-name = \"c\", \"python\", \"bash\", etc.

;; 3. Configure face colors for TS style IDs 1-19
(ts-setup-styles! ed)

;; 4. Force font on all 128 styles (no lexer to reset them)
(let loop ((i 0))
  (when (<= i 127)
    (sci-send/string ed SCI_STYLESETFONT *default-font-family* i)
    (sci-send ed SCI_STYLESETSIZE i *default-font-size*)
    (loop (+ i 1))))

;; 5. Initial parse + highlight
(let ((text (qt-plain-text-edit-text ed)))
  (when text (ts-buffer-reparse! buf text ed)))

;; KEY BENEFIT: Tree-sitter uses SCI_STARTSTYLING/SCI_SETSTYLING directly.
;; No setLexer() call = fonts are NEVER reset. Eliminates the recurring
;; font-size bug for all TS-supported languages.
notes: 14 grammars available: c, cpp, python, javascript, rust, go, bash, json, ruby, java, css, html, lua, scheme. The 150ms periodic reparse tick in app.ss auto-activates once ts-buffer-init! is called. ts-setup-styles! must read colors from the theme alist (theme-get) not from face-get — face-get can fail in static binaries.

title: Safe async text writes to Scintilla: switch to target document first
code:
;; PITFALL: qt-plain-text-edit-set-text! writes to whatever Scintilla
;; document is currently active on the editor widget. In async callbacks,
;; the user may have switched buffers, so the active document is WRONG.
;;
;; WRONG — writes into whatever buffer is currently displayed:
;; (async-read-file! path
;;   (lambda (text)
;;     (let ((ed (qt-current-editor fr)))
;;       (qt-plain-text-edit-set-text! ed text))))  ;; BUG!
;;
;; CORRECT — temporarily switch to the target document:
(let ((target-doc (buffer-doc-pointer target-buf)))
  (async-read-file! path
    (lambda (text)
      (let* ((ed (qt-current-editor (app-state-frame app)))
             (current-doc (sci-send ed SCI_GETDOCPOINTER 0)))
        ;; Switch to target document
        (sci-send ed SCI_SETDOCPOINTER 0 target-doc)
        (qt-plain-text-edit-set-text! ed text)
        ;; Switch back
        (sci-send ed SCI_SETDOCPOINTER 0 current-doc)))))
notes: Same pattern applies to ANY async or timer callback that writes text to a Scintilla editor. The vterm render path has a related issue: qt-app-process-events! can process switch-buffer events mid-render, changing the active document. Always re-check window-buffer association after processEvents.

title: Re-check mutable state after qt-app-process-events! (can trigger buffer switches)
code:
;; PITFALL: qt-app-process-events! pumps the Qt event queue, which can
;; fire key event callbacks. If a switch-buffer keystroke is queued, it
;; fires MID-OPERATION, changing the editor's active document.
;;
;; WRONG — assumes state is unchanged after processEvents:
;; (let ((ed (find-editor-for-terminal-buffer)))
;;   (vtscreen-feed! vt data)
;;   (qt-app-process-events! app)   ;; may fire switch-buffer!
;;   (qt-plain-text-edit-set-text! ed rendered))  ;; writes to WRONG doc!
;;
;; CORRECT — re-check after processEvents:
(let ((ed (qt-edit-window-editor win)))
  (vtscreen-feed! vt data)
  (qt-app-process-events! app)
  ;; Re-check: window may now show a different buffer
  (when (eq? (qt-edit-window-buffer win) terminal-buf)
    (qt-plain-text-edit-set-text! ed rendered)))
notes: This applies to ANY long-running operation that calls qt-app-process-events! for responsiveness. The processEvents call is necessary for UI responsiveness (M-x during vterm flood), but it introduces re-entrancy. Always re-validate mutable state after the call.

title: Case-sensitive key chords: use uppercase to avoid interfering with normal typing
code:
;; Key chords should be case-sensitive and use UPPERCASE characters.
;; This ensures lowercase typing in terminals has zero interference.
;;
;; Registration (case-sensitive, both orderings):
(def (key-chord-define-global two-char-str cmd)
  (let ((c1 (string-ref two-char-str 0))
        (c2 (string-ref two-char-str 1)))
    (hash-put! *chord-map* (string c1 c2) cmd)
    (when (not (char=? c1 c2))
      (hash-put! *chord-map* (string c2 c1) cmd))
    (hash-put! *chord-first-chars* c1 #t)
    (hash-put! *chord-first-chars* c2 #t)))

;; Example bindings (all uppercase):
;; chord MT vterm
;; chord EE eshell
;; chord GG keyboard-quit

;; IMPORTANT: During chord detection, IGNORE bare modifier key releases
;; (Shift, Ctrl, Alt, Meta). When typing uppercase TM, the Shift release
;; arrives between T and M. Without filtering, it cancels the chord.
;; Qt key codes for modifiers: #x01000020 to #x01000025, #x01000053, #x01000054
notes: The deferred callback queue drains every ~10ms. With a 100-200ms chord timeout, both chord keys must arrive within that window. The 10ms event loop (reduced from 50ms) ensures reliable chord detection. Bare modifier releases (Shift/Ctrl/Alt/Meta) must be ignored in Case 1 of the chord FSM, but real keys (M-x, C-s) must still cancel the chord and be processed normally.

title: Read git branch name from .git/HEAD without external commands
code:
(define (git-branch-name)
  (let loop ([dir (current-directory)])
    (let ([head-path (string-append dir "/.git/HEAD")])
      (if (file-exists? head-path)
          (guard (e [#t #f])
            (let* ([content (call-with-input-file head-path read-line)]
                   [prefix "ref: refs/heads/"])
              (if (and (string? content)
                       (>= (string-length content) (string-length prefix))
                       (string=? prefix (substring content 0 (string-length prefix))))
                  (substring content (string-length prefix) (string-length content))
                  ;; Detached HEAD — show short hash
                  (if (and (string? content) (>= (string-length content) 7))
                      (substring content 0 7)
                      #f))))
          ;; Walk up to parent directory
          (let ([parent (path-directory dir)])
            (if (or (not parent) (string=? parent dir) (string=? parent "/"))
                #f
                (loop parent)))))))
notes: Walks parent directories to find .git/HEAD (works in subdirs of a repo). Returns #f if not in a git repo. Handles both branch refs and detached HEAD (returns short 7-char hash). No subprocess spawned — pure file I/O. Useful for shell prompts where you want git info without forking git/grep/sed.

title: Post-build patch ordering: match text that exists at patch time
code:
;; WRONG: Patch 23 matches text added by Patch 30 (mux imports not yet applied)
(patch-file! "src/jsh/main.sls"
  "(jsh recorder)\n   (jsh mux-server)"          ; doesn't exist yet!
  "(jsh recorder)\n   (only (jsh prompt) git-branch-name)\n   (jsh mux-server)")

;; RIGHT: Match text that exists at this point in the patch sequence
(patch-file! "src/jsh/main.sls"
  "(jsh embed)\n   (jsh recorder))"               ; exists before mux patches
  "(jsh embed)\n   (only (jsh prompt) git-branch-name)\n   (jsh recorder))")
notes: When using sequential patch-file! calls in a build script, each patch transforms the file for subsequent patches. A patch that matches text introduced by a LATER patch will silently fail (no match found). Always verify your match string exists at the point in the sequence where your patch runs. Common gotcha: patches in different sections (e.g., recording section vs multiplexer section) run in file order, not logical grouping order.

title: Sort a list of symbols alphabetically (Chez Scheme)
code:
;; symbol<? does NOT exist in Chez Scheme. Use an inline comparator:
(list-sort (lambda (a b)
             (string<? (symbol->string a) (symbol->string b)))
           '(zebra apple mango banana))
;; => (apple banana mango zebra)

;; Common use: sort module exports for display
(let ([syms (environment-symbols (environment '(std sort)))])
  (list-sort (lambda (a b)
               (string<? (symbol->string a) (symbol->string b)))
             syms))
notes: symbol<? is NOT a Chez builtin — it does not exist. This is a common hallucination. Always use the lambda comparator with string<? and symbol->string. The same pattern works with list-sort from (chezscheme) or sort from (std sort).

title: list-of? and maybe are predicate factories (arity 1)
code:
;; list-of? takes ONE argument (the element predicate)
;; and RETURNS a predicate. It does NOT take the list directly.

;; WRONG: (list-of? number? '(1 2 3))   — arity error!
;; RIGHT:
((list-of? number?) '(1 2 3))    ;; => #t
((list-of? string?) '(1 2 3))    ;; => #f

;; maybe works the same way — predicate factory
((maybe string?) "hello")         ;; => #t
((maybe string?) #f)              ;; => #t  (accepts #f)
((maybe string?) 42)              ;; => #f

;; Use in contracts
(def (process-numbers (xs : (list-of? number?)))
  (apply + xs))
notes: list-of? and maybe from (std ergo) are predicate FACTORIES. They each take 1 argument (a predicate) and return a new predicate. Calling (list-of? pred lst) is a common mistake that gives a wrong-number-of-arguments error.

title: Introspect module exports at runtime with environment-symbols
code:
;; Get all symbols exported by a module
(let* ([mod-env (environment '(std sort))]
       [syms (environment-symbols mod-env)]
       [sorted (list-sort
                 (lambda (a b)
                   (string<? (symbol->string a) (symbol->string b)))
                 syms)])
  (for-each (lambda (s) (display s) (newline)) sorted))

;; Classify each export (procedure, value, macro)
(let ([mod-env (environment '(std result))])
  (for-each
    (lambda (sym)
      (guard (e [else (void)])
        (let ([val (eval sym mod-env)])
          (display sym)
          (display " : ")
          (display (cond [(procedure? val) "procedure"]
                         [(boolean? val) "boolean"]
                         [else "value"]))
          (newline))))
    (environment-symbols mod-env)))
notes: environment-symbols and environment are Chez builtins from (chezscheme). The module path must be quoted: (environment '(std sort)). Use eval with the module environment to inspect individual exports. Guard against macros which can't be eval'd.

title: QScintilla anti-flicker: buffered draw, two-phase, update batching, opaque paint
code:
;; Four techniques to reduce QScintilla flicker:

;; 1. Scintilla internal double-buffering (per editor, once at setup)
(sci-send ed 2035 1)  ; SCI_SETBUFFEREDDRAW = on
(sci-send ed 2284 1)  ; SCI_SETTWOPHASEDRAW = on

;; 2. WA_OpaquePaintEvent — prevent Qt from clearing background before paint
;; Qt::WA_OpaquePaintEvent = 4
(qt-widget-set-attribute! ed 4 #t)

;; 3. Batch multiple visual changes into one repaint
;; Use around buffer-attach, split-window, or any multi-step UI change
(qt-widget-set-updates-enabled! ed #f)
;; ... doc swap, highlight, font loop, margin colors ...
(qt-widget-set-updates-enabled! ed #t)  ;; single repaint here

;; 4. For window splits, suppress on the main window (not just editor)
(qt-widget-set-updates-enabled! main-win #f)
;; ... create widget, add to splitter, set sizes ...
(qt-widget-set-updates-enabled! main-win #t)
notes: Qt6 automatically double-buffers all widgets via QBackingStore, but Scintilla has its own internal layer. SCI_SETBUFFEREDDRAW adds Scintilla-level buffering on top. SCI_SETTWOPHASEDRAW renders background first then text, smoother for syntax-highlighted code. WA_OpaquePaintEvent eliminates the "white flash" when widgets are created/resized. setUpdatesEnabled is the most impactful — batch ALL related visual changes.

title: Measure actual character width in QScintilla (for terminal column calculation)
code:
;; PITFALL: Don't hardcode character width for terminal column calculations.
;; At different font sizes, character widths vary significantly.
;; Font 11pt → ~8px, Font 20pt → ~12px, Font 6pt → ~5px.
;;
;; WRONG — hardcoded pixel width:
;; (let ((cols (quotient widget-width 8)))  ;; breaks at non-default font
;;
;; CORRECT — measure actual width using Scintilla:
;; SCI_TEXTWIDTH(style, text) = 2275
(let* ((char-w (max 1 (sci-send/string ed 2275 "0" 0)))
       (cols (quotient widget-width char-w)))
  ;; cols now correct for any font size
  ...)
notes: SCI_TEXTWIDTH measures the pixel width of a string in a given style. Style 0 is the default. Use "0" (zero) as the measurement character since it's a standard monospace width. Always max with 1 to avoid division by zero. This is critical for terminal emulators where column count must match the visible width.

title: Vterm: use full set-text! for alt-screen programs (top, htop, vim)
code:
;; Alt-screen programs (top, htop, vim) refresh the entire screen.
;; Per-line row-diff rendering leaves gaps when rows aren't marked dirty
;; after screen clears (CSI 2J).
;;
;; WRONG — row-diff on alt-screen causes gaps:
;; (vterm-replace-line! ed (+ offset r) new-text)  ;; only dirty rows
;;
;; CORRECT — full set-text for alt-screen, row-diff for normal:
(if (vtscreen-alt-screen? vt)
  ;; Alt-screen: full render (no pre-PTY prefix)
  (begin
    (qt-widget-set-updates-enabled! ed #f)
    (qt-plain-text-edit-set-text! ed (vtscreen-render vt))
    (qt-widget-set-updates-enabled! ed #t))
  ;; Normal mode: per-line replacement for efficiency
  (vterm-row-diff-render! ed vt ts))
notes: The original reason for avoiding set-text! on alt-screen was visual bounce (scroll reset + cursor visible). The setUpdatesEnabled batching eliminates this. Full set-text! is more reliable because it doesn't depend on the vtscreen's dirty-row tracking, which can miss rows after screen clears.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: thread-sleep! replacement
code:
;; Gambit/Gerbil: (thread-sleep! 2)  — DOES NOT EXIST in Chez
;; Chez equivalent:
(sleep (make-time 'time-duration 0 2))  ;; sleep 2 seconds

;; For sub-second: nanoseconds first, then seconds
(sleep (make-time 'time-duration 500000000 0))  ;; sleep 0.5 seconds

;; Helper if you need it often:
(define (sleep-seconds n)
  (sleep (make-time 'time-duration 0 n)))
(define (sleep-ms ms)
  (sleep (make-time 'time-duration (* ms 1000000) 0)))
notes: thread-sleep! is Gambit. Chez's (sleep) takes a time object, not a number. The make-time args are: type, nanoseconds, seconds.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: read-line replacement
code:
;; Gambit/Gerbil: (read-line port)  — DOES NOT EXIST in Chez
;; Chez equivalent:
(get-line (current-input-port))  ;; read one line from stdin
(get-line port)                  ;; read one line from any port

;; Returns string without newline, or eof object
;; Check for EOF:
(let ((line (get-line port)))
  (if (eof-object? line)
      (displayln "end of input")
      (displayln "got: " line)))
notes: Gambit's read-line and Chez's get-line are functionally identical. Both strip the trailing newline.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: path-expand replacement
code:
;; Gerbil: (path-expand "file.txt" "/home/user")  — 2-arg version WRONG in Jerboa
;; Jerboa's path-expand takes 1 arg only.

;; For joining paths (the common 2-arg use case):
(path-join "/home/user" "file.txt")  ;; → "/home/user/file.txt"

;; For expanding ~ or relative paths (1-arg):
;; (path-expand "~/file.txt")  ;; if available in your prelude
notes: Gerbil's path-expand takes (rel base). Jerboa's takes 1 arg. The 2-arg use case is covered by path-join. This arity mismatch has caused errors across multiple sessions.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: hash table API translation
code:
;; WRONG (R6RS/Racket/Gerbil):        CORRECT (Jerboa):
;; (make-equal-hashtable)           → (make-hash-table)
;; (make-eqv-hashtable)             → (make-hash-table)  ; default is equal
;; (hash-table-set! ht k v)         → (hash-put! ht k v)
;; (hash-table-ref ht k)            → (hash-ref ht k)    ; or (hash-get ht k) for #f default
;; (hash-table-ref ht k default)    → (hash-ref ht k default)
;; (hash-table-delete! ht k)        → (hash-remove! ht k)
;; (hash-table-contains? ht k)      → (hash-key? ht k)
;; (hash-has-key? ht k)             → (hash-key? ht k)   ; Racket name
;; (hash-table-keys ht)             → (hash-keys ht)
;; (hash-table-values ht)           → (hash-values ht)
;; (hash-table->alist ht)           → (hash->list ht)
notes: Jerboa's hash API uses hash-put!/hash-ref/hash-get/hash-key?/hash-remove!. Both make-equal-hashtable (R6RS) and hash-has-key? (Racket) are common hallucinations. hash-get returns #f on missing key; hash-ref raises an error unless given a default.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: process-status and process API
code:
;; Gerbil: (process-status proc)  — DOES NOT EXIST in Chez
;; Gerbil: (open-process ...) — different API in Jerboa

;; Jerboa: use (std misc process)
(import (std misc process))

;; Run a command and get output:
(run-process/batch "ls" "-la")
;; Returns: (values stdout-string stderr-string exit-code)

;; Run and capture just stdout:
(let-values (((out err code) (run-process/batch "echo" "hello")))
  (displayln "output: " out)
  (displayln "exit code: " code))
notes: Gerbil's process API (open-process, process-status) does not exist in Chez. Jerboa provides run-process and run-process/batch in (std misc process). For low-level needs, Chez has open-process-ports.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: condition/report-string replacement
code:
;; Gerbil: (condition/report-string exn)  — DOES NOT EXIST in Chez
;; Chez equivalent:
(with-output-to-string
  (lambda () (display-condition exn)))

;; Example in a catch block:
(try
  (error 'test "something went wrong" 42)
  (catch (e)
    (let ((msg (with-output-to-string
                (lambda () (display-condition e)))))
      (displayln "Error: " msg))))
;; → Error: Exception in test: something went wrong
;;   42
notes: Gerbil's condition/report-string returns a human-readable string from a condition object. In Chez, use display-condition which writes to a port — wrap with with-output-to-string to get a string.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: misc API translations
code:
;; Quick reference for common Gerbil/Gambit→Chez translations:

;; (user-info-home (user-info (user-name)))  →  (getenv "HOME")
;; (the-environment)                         →  (interaction-environment)
;; (force-output port)                       →  (flush-output-port port)
;; (random-integer n)                        →  (random n)
;; (directory-exists? path)                  →  (file-directory? path)
;; (arithmetic-shift n k)                    →  (ash n k)  or (bitwise-arithmetic-shift n k)
;; (eql? a b)                                →  (eqv? a b)
;; (thread-yield!)                           →  (sleep (make-time 'time-duration 0 0))
;; (make-rwlock 'name)                       →  (make-rwlock)  ; 0 args in Jerboa!
;; (sort lst pred)                           →  (sort pred lst)  ; reversed arg order!
notes: Collected from 52 session transcripts. These are the most frequently hallucinated Gerbil/Gambit APIs. Each one has caused actual build failures in past sessions. The sort arg order reversal is especially common.

title: Gerbil→Jerboa: sort argument order (pred list, NOT list pred)
code:
;; WRONG (Gerbil/SRFI order):    (sort '(3 1 2) <)     ;; list first
;; CORRECT (Chez/Jerboa order):  (sort < '(3 1 2))     ;; predicate first!
;; → (1 2 3)

;; R6RS alternative (same order as Chez):
;; (list-sort < '(3 1 2))  → (1 2 3)

;; Sorting strings:
(sort string<? '("banana" "apple" "cherry"))
;; → ("apple" "banana" "cherry")

;; Sorting by a key:
(sort (lambda (a b) (< (cdr a) (cdr b)))
      '((x . 3) (y . 1) (z . 2)))
;; → ((y . 1) (z . 2) (x . 3))
notes: This is the #1 most confusing Gerbil→Chez difference. Gerbil uses (sort list pred), Chez uses (sort pred list). The Jerboa prelude re-exports Chez's sort. This has caused 80+ errors across sessions.

title: Lock current process to a directory with cage!
code:
;; Lock current process to a directory (IRREVERSIBLE)
;; Network access is preserved. Child processes inherit the cage.

;; Minimal — lock to project dir with auto-detected system paths
(cage! (make-cage-config 'root: "/home/user/project"))

;; Full options
(cage! (make-cage-config
  'root: "/home/user/project"      ;; read-write root (required)
  'read-only: '("/usr/share/man")  ;; extra read-only paths
  'read-write: '("/tmp/scratch")   ;; extra read-write paths
  'execute: '("/opt/bin")          ;; extra execute paths
  'network: #t                     ;; allow network (default #t)
  'system-paths: 'auto             ;; auto-detect runtime needs (default)
  'temp-dir: "/tmp"))              ;; writable temp (default: /tmp)

;; Check cage state
(cage-active?)        ;; → #t after cage!
(cage-root)           ;; → resolved root path
(cage-allowed-paths)  ;; → alist of (mode . path)
notes: Keywords must be quoted in .ss files: 'root: not root: (Jerboa reader turns root: into #:root keyword which isn't self-evaluating). cage! uses Landlock on Linux — requires kernel 5.13+. There is no uncage! — start a new process to escape. system-paths: 'auto includes /usr/lib, /lib, /etc/ssl, /dev/urandom, /proc, terminal paths, etc.

title: Use void* (not u8*) for foreign-alloc'd output pointer params in foreign-procedure
code:
;; WRONG — u8* expects a bytevector, not a foreign-alloc'd pointer:
;; (define c-compile (foreign-procedure \"my_compile\" (u8* size_t u8*) int))
;;                                                              ^^^
;; Passing (foreign-alloc 8) to a u8* param causes:
;;   \"invalid foreign-procedure argument 140234567890\"

;; CORRECT — use void* for foreign-alloc'd output buffers:
(define c-compile
  (foreign-procedure \"my_compile\"
    (u8* size_t void*) int))
;;  ^^^         ^^^^
;;  input bv    output pointer

;; Usage:
(let ([input-bv (string->utf8 \"hello\")]
      [out-buf  (foreign-alloc 8)])
  (let ([rc (c-compile input-bv (bytevector-length input-bv) out-buf)])
    (let ([result (foreign-ref 'unsigned-64 out-buf 0)])
      (foreign-free out-buf)
      result)))

;; Rule of thumb:
;;   u8*   → pass a Scheme bytevector (string->utf8, make-bytevector)
;;   void* → pass a foreign-alloc'd raw pointer (fixnum)
notes: This is a common mistake when writing FFI wrappers for functions that take both input bytevectors and output pointer buffers. The u8* type in Chez foreign-procedure expects a Scheme bytevector object — passing a foreign-alloc integer causes 'invalid foreign-procedure argument'. Use void* for any parameter that receives a raw pointer from foreign-alloc. The same applies to size_t output parameters.

title: Wrap a (main args-list) function as a shell builtin (non-variadic entry point)
code:
;; Problem: make-coreutils-builtin uses (apply main-fn args) which spreads
;; the arg list. This works for variadic (define (main . args) ...) but
;; breaks for (define (main args) ...) which expects a single list argument.
;;
;; For variadic main (most coreutils):
;;   (apply main-fn '(\"--flag\" \"file\"))  =>  (main \"--flag\" \"file\")  ✓
;;
;; For single-list main (e.g. jerboa-awk):
;;   (apply main-fn '(\"'{print}'\"))  =>  (main \"'{print}'\")  ✗ (string, not list)
;;   (main-fn '(\"'{print}'\"))        =>  (main '(\"'{print}'\"))  ✓

;; Wrapper variant for single-list-arg main functions:
(define (make-builtin/list name main-fn)
  (lambda (args env)
    (let ((status
           (call/cc
            (lambda (k)
              (parameterize ([exit-handler (lambda (code) (k code))])
                (guard (e [#t
                  (guard (e2 [#t (values)])
                    (display-condition e (current-error-port))
                    (newline (current-error-port)))
                  1])
                  (main-fn args)   ;; ← direct call, NOT apply
                  0))))))
      (if (fixnum? status) status 0))))
notes: When integrating external command modules as shell builtins, check the main function signature. Gerbil-style (def (main . args)) is variadic and works with apply. But some modules use (define (main args)) taking a single list. Using apply on a single-list main causes the list elements to become individual arguments, triggering 'not a pair' errors when the function tries to process them as a list. The fix is to call (main-fn args) directly instead of (apply main-fn args).

title: Replace C PCRE2 with Rust regex via jerboa-native-rs FFI
code:
;; Rust FFI functions (from jerboa-native-rs/src/regex_native.rs):
;;   jerboa_regex_compile_ex(pattern, len, flags, &handle) -> int
;;   jerboa_regex_find_at(handle, text, len, start, &match_start, &match_end) -> int
;;   jerboa_regex_captures(handle, text, len, start, ovector, capacity) -> int
;;   jerboa_regex_group_count(handle) -> int
;;   jerboa_regex_free(handle) -> int

;; PCRE2 flag bitmask (maps to Rust inline flags):
;;   0x8     = CASELESS   -> (?i)
;;   0x20    = DOTALL     -> (?s)
;;   0x400   = MULTILINE  -> (?m)
;;   0x80000 = UTF        -> (ignored, Rust is always UTF-8)

;; Scheme declarations — note void* for output pointer params:
(define c-regex-compile-ex
  (foreign-procedure \"jerboa_regex_compile_ex\"
    (u8* size_t unsigned-32 void*) int))

(define c-regex-find-at
  (foreign-procedure \"jerboa_regex_find_at\"
    (unsigned-64 u8* size_t size_t void* void*) int))

(define c-regex-captures
  (foreign-procedure \"jerboa_regex_captures\"
    (unsigned-64 u8* size_t size_t void* size_t) int))

;; Compile pattern:
(define (pcre2-compile pattern flags)
  (let ([handle-buf (foreign-alloc 8)])
    (let ([rc (c-regex-compile-ex
                (string->utf8 pattern)
                (string-length pattern)
                flags
                handle-buf)])
      (if (< rc 0)
        (begin (foreign-free handle-buf)
               (error 'pcre2-compile \"failed\" pattern))
        (let ([h (foreign-ref 'unsigned-64 handle-buf 0)])
          (foreign-free handle-buf) h)))))
notes: This pattern replaces C PCRE2 (libpcre2-8) with the Rust regex crate already in libjerboa_native.a. The key advantage is eliminating a C dependency in static musl builds. The Rust regex crate doesn't support backreferences or lookahead/lookbehind (unlike PCRE2), but handles the vast majority of grep/sed use cases. PCRE2 flag constants are mapped to Rust inline flag syntax in the compile_ex function. For static builds, register the 4 new symbols via Sforeign_symbol in the C entry point.

title: Use gmake (GNU Make) on FreeBSD for Jerboa projects
code:
# FreeBSD ships BSD make as /usr/bin/make, which is NOT GNU make.
# Jerboa project Makefiles use GNU make syntax:
#   $(CURDIR)        — BSD make uses .CURDIR instead
#   $(wildcard ...)  — no BSD make equivalent
#   $< in explicit rules — BSD make only supports $< in suffix rules
#
# Always use gmake on FreeBSD:
gmake build
gmake binary
gmake clean
gmake test
notes: FreeBSD's default `make` is BSD make, which is incompatible with GNU make syntax used in Jerboa Makefiles. GNU make is available as `gmake` via the `devel/gmake` package (`pkg install gmake`). Common symptoms of using BSD make: 'Invalid character in variable name' for $(wildcard ...), 'don't know how to make' errors from unresolved $(CURDIR), and empty $< in explicit target rules.

title: Set CHEZ_DIR on FreeBSD for native binary builds
code:
# The build-binary.ss script needs CHEZ_DIR to find petite.boot,
# scheme.boot, main.o, and libkernel.a.
#
# It auto-detects ~/.local/lib/csv*/  but on FreeBSD, Chez is
# typically installed to /usr/local/lib/ by the pkg system.
#
# Find your Chez install:
ls /usr/local/lib/csv*/
# e.g. /usr/local/lib/csv10.3.0/ta6fb/
#
# Verify it has the required files:
ls /usr/local/lib/csv10.3.0/ta6fb/{main.o,petite.boot,scheme.boot,scheme.h}
#
# Build with CHEZ_DIR:
CHEZ_DIR=/usr/local/lib/csv10.3.0/ta6fb gmake binary
notes: The build-binary.ss script only auto-detects Chez in ~/.local/lib/csv*/<machine-type>/. On FreeBSD (pkg install chez-scheme), Chez installs to /usr/local/lib/csv<version>/ta6fb/. The machine-type directory (ta6fb = threaded, a6 = amd64, fb = FreeBSD) varies by platform. Set CHEZ_DIR to the directory containing main.o, petite.boot, scheme.boot, and scheme.h.

title: Link a static Chez Scheme binary on FreeBSD (no musl)
notes: FreeBSD static builds do NOT need musl or glibc-compat shims. The system libc (FreeBSD libc) links statically cleanly. Key gotchas: (1) __errno_location is Linux-specific — must provide a wrapper. (2) /proc/self/fd/ and /proc/self/exe don't exist — use /dev/fd/ and sysctl KERN_PROC_PATHNAME. (3) sed -i requires '' as backup extension argument on FreeBSD. (4) Use -lthr not -lpthread on FreeBSD. (5) No -ldl needed (dlopen is in libc). (6) Chez platform is ta6fb (FreeBSD amd64), not ta6le (Linux).

title: Build Chez Scheme from source on FreeBSD
code:
# FreeBSD requires gmake — BSD make cannot parse lz4's GNU Makefile
# (produces 'Invalid line "ifeq ..."' errors)
pkg install gmake

# Configure with absolute installprefix (relative paths fail)
./configure --installprefix=/home/user/chez-local --threads

# Build and install using gmake
gmake -j$(sysctl -n hw.ncpu)
zuo ta6fb install        # or: gmake install (for older workareas)

# With security hardening (--enable-harden):
./configure --installprefix=/home/user/chez-secure --threads --enable-harden
# Adds: -fstack-protector-strong -fstack-clash-protection -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2
#       -fPIC (CFLAGS), -pie (mdlinkflags only), -Wl,-z,relro,-z,now (LDFLAGS)
# On x86_64 Linux: also adds -fcf-protection=full (Intel CET)
# On arm64: also adds -mbranch-protection=standard (ARM PAC+BTI)
# Note: -pie goes in mdlinkflags, NOT LDFLAGS — LDFLAGS is inherited by
#       ftype tests that compile shared objects and -pie conflicts with -shared

# After configure, if you add --enable-harden and rebuild,
# you must clean C objects manually (build system may not re-detect flag changes):
find ta6fb/c -name "*.o" -delete
rm -f ta6fb/boot/ta6fb/main.o ta6fb/boot/ta6fb/libkernel.a
rm -rf ta6fb/zlib ta6fb/lz4
gmake

# Verify hardening on the resulting binary:
file ta6fb/bin/ta6fb/scheme           # should say "pie executable"
readelf -d ta6fb/bin/ta6fb/scheme | grep -i "flags\|bind_now"
# FLAGS: BIND_NOW  and  FLAGS_1: NOW PIE  confirms full RELRO + ASLR

# Machine type on FreeBSD amd64: ta6fb (threaded, a6=amd64, fb=FreeBSD)
notes: lz4's Makefile uses GNU make conditionals (ifeq/else/endif) that BSD make rejects with 'Invalid line' errors. Always use gmake. The --enable-harden flag was added to the secure branch of ChezScheme (~/ChezScheme). The -pie vs -shared conflict: -pie in LDFLAGS breaks ftype tests which compile .so files using the same LDFLAGS; fix is to put -pie in mdlinkflags which is only used for the scheme executable link, not inherited by test compilation. Use -fPIC (not -fPIE) in CFLAGS since ftype tests also inherit CFLAGS and need -fPIC for shared object compilation.

title: Avoid (jerboa prelude) in .ss files when jerbuild causes multiple-definition conflicts
code:
;; PROBLEM: jerbuild auto-adds (jerboa core) and (jerboa runtime) to all .sls imports.
;; If the updated (jerboa prelude) re-exports symbols from (jerboa core) (e.g. match),
;; using (import (jerboa prelude)) in .ss files causes "multiple definitions" errors
;; because jerbuild adds (jerboa core) AND your .ss imports (jerboa prelude) which
;; re-exports the same symbols.
;;
;; SOLUTION: Import individual std modules instead of (jerboa prelude):

(import
  (std sugar)          ;; def, defrule, try/catch, etc.
  (std sort)           ;; sort
  (except (std srfi srfi-13) string-pad-right)  ;; string-join, string-prefix?, etc.
  ;; Add only what you need:
  ;; (std text json)   ;; JSON parsing
  ;; (std misc string) ;; string-empty?, etc.
  ;; (std misc ports)  ;; read-file-lines, etc.
)

;; NOTE: (except ...) is needed when a module re-exports something
;; already provided by jerbuild's auto-added (jerboa core).
;; Common conflicts: string-pad-right, match
notes: This only affects .ss files processed by jerbuild. Direct .sls files don't have this issue. The conflict appears when (jerboa prelude) is updated to re-export new symbols from (jerboa core). If you see 'multiple definitions for X', check if X is in both (jerboa core) and the module you're importing.

title: Build Rust static library with rustup when Homebrew rustc shadows it
code:
;; PROBLEM: When both Homebrew (linuxbrew) and rustup install Rust,
;; Homebrew's rustc appears first in PATH. It lacks the musl target
;; even though `rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl` succeeded
;; (rustup installs to its own toolchain, not Homebrew's).
;;
;; SYMPTOM: error[E0463]: can't find crate for `core`
;;          note: the `x86_64-unknown-linux-musl` target may not be installed
;;
;; DIAGNOSIS:
;;   which rustc          → /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/rustc (WRONG)
;;   rustup which rustc   → ~/.rustup/toolchains/stable-.../bin/rustc (RIGHT)
;;
;; FIX: Set RUSTC explicitly to use rustup's toolchain:
;;
;; RUSTC=~/.rustup/toolchains/stable-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/bin/rustc \
;;   ~/.rustup/toolchains/stable-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/bin/cargo \
;;   build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
notes: This affects any system with both Homebrew and rustup installed. The musl stdlib files exist at ~/.rustup/toolchains/stable-.../lib/rustlib/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/lib/ but Homebrew's rustc can't find them. Always verify with `which rustc` vs `rustup which rustc` when musl builds fail.

title: Docker creates directories when volume-mounting nonexistent files
notes: This is a well-known Docker footgun. Always check that volume-mount source paths for files (not directories) actually exist before running docker. Root-owned artifacts left behind require Docker or sudo to clean up.

title: thread-sleep! in standalone prelude scripts (capture shadowed make-time)
code:
;; The (jerboa prelude) shadows Chez's make-time with a datetime constructor.
;; Capture the original BEFORE the prelude can shadow it, using a local import.

(def chez:make-time
  (let () (import (only (chezscheme) make-time)) make-time))

(def (thread-sleep! secs)
  "Sleep for SECS seconds (real number). Works in standalone Jerboa prelude scripts."
  (let* ((diff (max 0 secs))
         (s  (exact (floor diff)))
         (ns (exact (floor (* (- diff s) 1000000000)))))
    (sleep (chez:make-time 'time-duration ns s))))

;; Usage:
(thread-sleep! 0.5)   ;; sleep 500ms
(thread-sleep! 2)     ;; sleep 2 seconds
notes: The (jerboa prelude) re-exports `make-time` as a datetime constructor, shadowing the Chez `make-time` that `sleep` requires. The `(let () (import (only (chezscheme) make-time)) make-time)` trick captures the original binding before shadowing occurs — the same technique used in `chez-srfis/srfi/srfi-18.sls`. Note: arg order to `make-time` is `(make-time type nanoseconds seconds)`. Without this workaround, `(sleep (make-time 'time-duration 0 1))` fails because prelude's `make-time` expects a year/month/day signature.

title: Scintilla dynamic style allocation for 256-color/truecolor terminal rendering
code:
;; Scintilla has 256 styles (0-255). Layout for terminal use:
;;   0-63  : editor/syntax styles
;;   64-79 : 16 pre-allocated ANSI colors (set up at terminal init)
;;   80-249 : dynamically allocated for (fg . bg) pairs — 170 slots
;;   250-255 : reserve

(def *vterm-next-style* 80)
(def *vterm-color-to-style* (make-hash-table))  ;; (fg . bg) -> style-id
(def *vterm-max-styles* 250)
(def *term-default-bg* #x181818)  ;; terminal background
(def *term-default-fg* #xc5c8c6)  ;; terminal default fg (for reverse-video)

(def (vterm-get-or-alloc-style! ed fg bg)
  "Get or allocate a Scintilla style for a (fg, bg) color pair.
   fg and bg are packed 0x00RRGGBB or -1 for default.
   Returns style index, or 0 if out of slots."
  (let ((key (cons fg bg)))
    (or (hash-ref *vterm-color-to-style* key #f)
        (if (>= *vterm-next-style* *vterm-max-styles*)
          0  ;; fallback to default style
          (let ((style *vterm-next-style*))
            (set! *vterm-next-style* (+ style 1))
            (when (not (= fg -1))
              (sci-send ed SCI_STYLESETFORE style fg))
            (sci-send ed SCI_STYLESETBACK style
                      (if (= bg -1) *term-default-bg* bg))
            (hash-put! *vterm-color-to-style* key style)
            style)))))

;; Per-cell rendering loop (in vterm-apply-row-colors!):
;; Read both fg and bg per cell, handle reverse-video attribute (bit 4).
(let* ((fg    (vtscreen-cell-fg vt row c))    ;; packed 0x00RRGGBB or -1
       (bg    (vtscreen-cell-bg vt row c))    ;; packed 0x00RRGGBB or -1
       (attrs (vtscreen-cell-attrs vt row c)) ;; packed bits — see notes
       (bold?    (not (= 0 (bitwise-and attrs 1))))
       (reverse? (not (= 0 (bitwise-and attrs 16))))
       ;; Reverse video: swap fg/bg, substituting defaults with concrete colors
       (eff-fg (if reverse? (if (= bg -1) *term-default-bg* bg) fg))
       (eff-bg (if reverse? (if (= fg -1) *term-default-fg* fg) bg))
       (style
        (cond
          ((and (= eff-fg -1) (= eff-bg -1))
           (if bold? (+ *term-style-base* 15) 0))
          (else
           (vterm-get-or-alloc-style! ed eff-fg eff-bg)))))
  ...)
notes:
libvterm already converts indexed (256-color) and RGB (truecolor) to packed 0x00RRGGBB via vterm_screen_convert_color_to_rgb in the C shim — vtscreen-cell-fg/bg always return resolved RGB values, never raw indices.

Cell attribute bit positions (from vterm_shim.c jvt_get_cell_attrs):
  bit 0: bold
  bit 1: underline (any type)
  bit 2: italic
  bit 3: blink
  bit 4: reverse video
  bit 5: strikethrough
  bit 6: conceal

Key on (cons fg bg) not just fg — styles encode BOTH fg and bg in Scintilla. 170 slots covers all 256 indexed colors plus typical RGB usage without collision.

Multi-editor caveat: *vterm-color-to-style* is global, so style 85 allocated for editor A will be returned for editor B without re-configuring it on B. Fine for single-terminal use; for multiple terminals consider tracking configured (editor . style) pairs.

title: jerbuild defstruct: positional constructors only, no transparent:
code:
;; WRONG — jerbuild defstruct does NOT support transparent: keyword:
;; (defstruct point (x y) transparent: #t)   ;; ERROR at compile time

;; CORRECT — just omit it:
(defstruct point (x y))

;; WRONG — jerbuild generates POSITIONAL constructors, not keyword ones.
;; Keyword args like (make-point x: 1 y: 2) get quoted as extra positional
;; args, causing "incorrect argument count" at runtime:
;; (make-point x: 1 y: 2)   ;; ERROR: passes 4 args instead of 2

;; CORRECT — always use positional args:
(def p (make-point 1 2))   ;; x=1, y=2

;; When initializing a struct with many fields, use comments for clarity:
(defstruct state (columns entries filtered sort-col sort-asc? filter-str padding))

(make-state
  columns    ;; columns
  '()        ;; entries
  '()        ;; filtered
  #f         ;; sort-col
  #t         ;; sort-asc?
  #f         ;; filter-str
  2)         ;; padding
notes: Two jerbuild-specific defstruct limitations that cause runtime errors: (1) transparent: keyword is silently ignored or causes build errors — always remove it. (2) The generated constructor is positional-only. Calling (make-foo key: val ...) makes jerbuild quote each keyword as a symbol and pass it as a positional arg, resulting in 2x the expected argument count and an 'incorrect argument count' error at runtime. This is not obvious because Gerbil's defstruct supports keyword constructors.

title: (std sort) import shadows prelude's sort with Gerbil arg order
code:
;; The prelude's sort uses Chez order: (sort predicate list)
(sort < '(3 1 2))  ;; → (1 2 3)  CORRECT

;; DANGER: importing (std sort) shadows the prelude's sort with
;; Gerbil/SRFI order: (sort list predicate)
;; This causes a runtime crash — the list is passed as the comparator:
;;
;; (import (std sort))
;; (sort < '(3 1 2))
;; → Exception in list-sort: (3 1 2) is not a procedure
;;
;; The error is confusing because the list appears in the "not a procedure"
;; message — it's being used as the predicate arg to the underlying list-sort.

;; FIX: Never import (std sort) unless you deliberately want Gerbil order
;; and flip all call sites to (sort list predicate).
;; In .ss files processed by jerbuild, just use the prelude's sort directly.

;; If you see "is not a procedure" in list-sort with a list as the argument,
;; check all imports for (std sort) or :std/sort shadowing the prelude.
notes: This caused a silent UI-QUEUE-ERROR in a tabulated-list sort — the callback crashed silently and the buffer just stayed stuck showing "Fetching...". The error message 'Exception in list-sort: (...list of items...) is not a procedure' is the key signature: the list appears where a procedure is expected because (std sort) swapped the args. The fix is to remove the (std sort) import entirely and rely on the prelude's sort.

title: Native SHA256 and HMAC-SHA256 with std/crypto/native
code:
;; SHA256 hash of string or bytevector
(import (std crypto native))

(define (sha256 data)
  (native-digest 'sha256
    (if (string? data) (string->utf8 data) data)))

;; HMAC-SHA256 — key and data can be strings or bytevectors
(define (hmac-sha256 key data)
  (native-hmac-sha256
    (if (string? key)  (string->utf8 key)  key)
    (if (string? data) (string->utf8 data) data)))

;; Returns bytevectors. To get hex string:
(define (bytes->hex bv)
  (apply string-append
    (map (lambda (b) (format "~2,'0x" b))
         (bytevector->u8-list bv))))

(bytes->hex (sha256 "hello world"))
;; => "b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe04294e576fbc9a08f"...
notes: Use this instead of shelling out to `openssl dgst`. The subprocess approach (open-process-ports "openssl dgst -sha256 ...") fails in static binaries and is vulnerable to PATH manipulation. native-digest and native-hmac-sha256 use EVP FFI directly — zero subprocess overhead, works in static builds. This is how jerboa-aws/crypto should be replaced for static build compatibility (see vendor/jerboa-aws-crypto-native.sls pattern).

title: Fetch EC2 instances with native jerboa-aws (no subprocess)
code:
;; Create a per-region EC2 client and fetch running instances
(import (jerboa-aws ec2 api))
(import (jerboa-aws ec2 instances))

;; Create client for a region
(def client (EC2Client 'region: "us-east-1"))

;; Call describe-instances — returns parsed response as Chez hashtables
;; Keys are Chez symbols (not strings): 'instanceId, 'instanceType, etc.
(def response (describe-instances client))

;; Navigate the response structure:
;;   response → hashtable with 'reservationSet
;;   reservationSet → list of reservations
;;   each reservation → 'instancesSet → list of instance hashtables
(def (parse-running-instances response)
  (let ((reservation-set (hashtable-ref response 'reservationSet '())))
    (filter-map
      (lambda (reservation)
        (let* ((instances-set (hashtable-ref reservation 'instancesSet '()))
               (instances (if (list? instances-set) instances-set (list instances-set))))
          (filter
            (lambda (inst)
              (let* ((state (hashtable-ref inst 'instanceState #f))
                     (name  (and state (hashtable-ref state 'name #f))))
                (equal? name "running")))
            instances)))
      (if (list? reservation-set)
          reservation-set
          (list reservation-set)))))

;; Get a tag value from an instance
(def (instance-tag inst tag-name)
  (let ((tag-set (hashtable-ref inst 'tagSet '())))
    (let loop ((tags (if (list? tag-set) tag-set (list tag-set))))
      (cond
        ((null? tags) #f)
        ((equal? (hashtable-ref (car tags) 'key #f) tag-name)
         (hashtable-ref (car tags) 'value #f))
        (else (loop (cdr tags)))))))

;; Example: get Name tag
(def instances (parse-running-instances response))
(for-each
  (lambda (inst)
    (displayln (hashtable-ref inst 'instanceId "?")
               " " (or (instance-tag inst "Name") "-")))
  instances)
notes: All hashtable keys are Chez symbols, not strings. Use hashtable-ref (not hash-ref) because these are native Chez equal-hashtables. reservationSet and instancesSet may be a single hash (not a list) when there's exactly one item — always normalize with (if (list? x) x (list x)). tagSet has the same single-vs-list issue. Credentials come from AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY / AWS_SESSION_TOKEN env vars or ~/.aws/credentials. The EC2Client constructor takes 'region: keyword arg.

title: Background worker delivering results to jemacs UI thread via ui-queue-push!
code:
;; jemacs pattern: spawn background work, deliver result back to UI thread
;; spawn-worker: runs thunk in a background thread (non-blocking)
;; ui-queue-push!: schedules a thunk to run on the Qt/UI main thread

;; Counter for pending work (modified only from UI thread)
(def *pending-count* 0)
(def *results* '())

(def (fetch-all-items! items callback)
  (set! *pending-count* (length items))
  (set! *results* '())
  (for-each
    (lambda (item)
      (spawn-worker 'fetch-item
        (lambda ()
          ;; This runs on a background thread — can do blocking I/O
          (with-catch
            (lambda (e)
              ;; On error: return to UI thread to decrement counter
              (ui-queue-push!
                (lambda ()
                  (set! *pending-count* (- *pending-count* 1))
                  (check-complete! callback))))
            (lambda ()
              (let ((result (do-blocking-work item)))
                ;; Deliver result to UI thread
                (ui-queue-push!
                  (lambda ()
                    (set! *results* (cons result *results*))
                    (set! *pending-count* (- *pending-count* 1))
                    (check-complete! callback))))))))
    items))

(def (check-complete! callback)
  ;; Called from UI thread — safe to access UI state
  (when (= *pending-count* 0)
    (callback *results*)))
notes: CRITICAL: *pending-count* and *results* must only be read/written from within ui-queue-push! thunks (UI thread). Never mutate shared state directly from spawn-worker — only from ui-queue-push! callbacks. with-catch around the background work ensures the counter decrements even on error, preventing a permanent "loading" state. This pattern drives aws-ec2-ssh mode fetching EC2 instances across 16 regions in parallel.

title: Override a third-party module for static binary compatibility via vendor/
code:
;; Pattern: vendor/chez-ssl-static.sls
;; Copy the upstream module's .sls to vendor/, then guard load-shared-object
;; so the static binary skips it (symbols already registered via Sforeign_symbol).

;; In vendor/chez-ssl-static.sls (library declaration matches original):
;; (library (chez-ssl) ...)
;;   ...same body as upstream...
;;   BUT replace:
;;     (load-shared-object "libssl.so")
;;     (load-shared-object "libcrypto.so")
;;     (load-shared-object "chez_ssl_shim.so")
;;   WITH:

(define load-libs
  (let ([static (getenv "JEMACS_STATIC")])
    (if (and static
             (not (string=? static ""))
             (not (string=? static "0")))
      (void)   ;; static binary: symbols pre-registered, skip load
      (begin
        (load-shared-object "libssl.so")
        (load-shared-object "libcrypto.so")
        (load-shared-object "chez_ssl_shim.so")))))

;; The static binary sets JEMACS_STATIC=1 in the build script.
;; The override file lives in vendor/ which comes BEFORE the upstream
;; lib dir in --libdirs, so it shadows the original.

;; In Makefile, add vendor/ to LIBDIRS before the upstream path:
;;   LIBDIRS = vendor:$(CSSL):...
;;
;; In Docker container setup, copy the override into place:
;;   cp vendor/chez-ssl-static.sls /deps/chez-ssl/src/chez-ssl.sls
notes: Use this when a third-party library calls load-shared-object at module load time and you can't modify the upstream source. The override file is only compiled into the Docker container — development mode still uses the upstream. The JEMACS_STATIC check is a 3-way guard: (and static (not empty) (not "0")) so unsetting the var reverts to dynamic loading. Alpine's openssl-dev only ships shared .so files — if you need static libssl.a, you must build OpenSSL from source inside the container. See also: chez-load-shared-object-static for the simpler guard-only pattern.

title: Run a comprehensive security audit using parallel MCP tools
code:
;; When auditing a Jerboa project, run all 5 security tools IN PARALLEL
;; for maximum coverage. Each tool catches different classes of issues.
;;
;; Step 1: Run these 5 MCP tools simultaneously (single message, parallel calls):
;;
;;   jerboa_security_audit    — Rules: eval-injection, deserialization,
;;                              sanitization, taint, capability, injection
;;   jerboa_import_policy_check — Detects (import (chezscheme)),
;;                              inline foreign-procedure, load-shared-object
;;   jerboa_unsafe_import_lint  — Warns on unsafe raw APIs, suggests safe-* wrappers
;;   jerboa_resource_leak_check — Finds open-*-file, sqlite-open, tcp-*
;;                              without with-resource/unwind-protect cleanup
;;   jerboa_safe_prelude_check  — Counts unsafe API calls (system, bare read,
;;                              open-input-file) and suggests safe alternatives
;;
;; Step 2: For C/FFI files, also run:
;;   jerboa_security_scan     — Rules for bare-read-untrusted, missing-eintr-retry,
;;                              direct-chezscheme-import, foreign-alloc-no-free,
;;                              ftype-buffer-overflow, load-shared-object-crash-static
;;
;; Step 3: Prioritize findings:
;;   CRITICAL = code execution (eval injection, bare read, FASL deser)
;;   HIGH = data exposure (path traversal, missing taint, shell injection)
;;   MEDIUM = reliability (resource leaks, unchecked dimensions)
;;   LOW = best practices (bare error, unstructured conditions)
;;
;; Step 4: Write report to PROJECT_DIR/security-report-DATE.md with:
;;   - Executive summary (total counts by severity)
;;   - Per-category sections with file:line references
;;   - Positive security measures observed
;;   - Prioritized recommendations (immediate/short-term/medium-term)
;;   - Scope limitations (what was NOT tested)
notes: The key insight is running ALL 5 tools in a SINGLE message with parallel tool calls. This is ~3x faster than sequential and catches complementary issue classes. jerboa_security_audit is the broadest (12 rules, 1000+ findings typical). jerboa_resource_leak_check catches the most actionable issues for production use. jerboa_import_policy_check is essential for projects that enforce the Jerboa capability system. Always review C files manually for EINTR, explicit_bzero usage, and memfd sealing — these are not caught by the Scheme-level tools.

title: Analyze Jerboa language for AI coding assistance
code:
(def (analyze-jerboa-for-ai)
  (displayln "Jerboa strengths: concise macros, prelude, Rust FFI.")
  (displayln "Suggestions: Add ML crates via native backend, AI code gen tools."))
notes: Use for reviewing Jerboa ecosystem; extend with semantic search over stdlib.

title: (std debug timetravel) — Time-Travel Recorder API
code:
(import (jerboa prelude))
(import (std debug timetravel))

;; Create a recorder and use it
(def rec (make-recorder))

;; Option 1: manual start/stop
(recorder-start! rec)
(record-event! rec 'step "began")
(record-call!   rec 'my-fn '(1 2))
(record-return! rec 'my-fn 42)
(record-state!  rec 'x 10)
(record-snapshot! rec 'checkpoint "v1")
(recorder-stop! rec)

;; Option 2: macro — starts/stops with dynamic-wind, returns (values result rec)
(define-values (result rec2)
  (with-recording (make-recorder)
    (record-event! rec2 'start "now")  ; rec2 bound inside body
    99))

;; Inspect events
(displayln (recorder-event-count rec))   ; number of recorded events
(displayln (recorder-events rec))        ; list oldest-first

;; Event accessors
(let ((ev (car (recorder-events rec))))
  (displayln (event-tag ev))             ; symbol tag
  (displayln (event-data ev))            ; arbitrary data
  (displayln (event-step ev))            ; sequential step number
  (displayln (event-timestamp ev)))      ; millisecond timestamp

;; Replay
(replay-events (recorder-events rec)
  (lambda (ev) (displayln "replaying: " (event-tag ev))))

;; Replay to step N — returns the last *state* value at or before step N
(displayln (replay-to-step (recorder-events rec) 2))

;; Filter helpers
(def ts-events  (events-between (recorder-events rec) 0 99999))  ; by ms range
(def step-evs   (events-by-tag  (recorder-events rec) 'step))
(def snap       (find-snapshot  (recorder-events rec) 'checkpoint)) ; most recent
(def all-snaps  (snapshots-for  (recorder-events rec) 'checkpoint)) ; all in order

;; Diff two events
(displayln (event-diff (car (recorder-events rec)) (cadr (recorder-events rec))))

;; Auto-instrument a function
(def traced-add (trace-fn rec +))
(traced-add 1 2)   ; records call + return automatically

;; Reset without replacing recorder
(recorder-reset! rec)
(displayln (recorder-event-count rec))  ; 0
notes: make-recorder takes 0 args. with-recording is a macro that wraps body in dynamic-wind start/stop and returns (values result rec).
