;; HTTP handler (after actor spawned and ID captured):
(def (handle-cluster-info req)
  (respond-json 200
    (json-object->string
      (list->hash-table
        `(("node_id"         . ,(or (current-node-id) "standalone"))
          ("ingest_actor_id" . ,(or *ingest-actor-id* 0)))))))

;; == On startup: connect to peers ==

(def *peer-refs* '())

(def (connect-to-peers! peer-http-addrs)
  ;; peer-http-addrs: list of "host:port" strings for HTTP endpoints of peers
  ;; Must be called AFTER local HTTP server is up (so peers can call us too)
  (let ([refs
         (filter-map
           (lambda (peer-http)
             (guard (exn [#t
                          (displayln "peer connect failed: " peer-http)
                          #f])
               (let* ([url  (format "http://~a/api/cluster/info" peer-http)]
                      [resp (http-get url)]
                      [json (string->json-object (request-text resp))]
                      [id   (hash-ref json "ingest_actor_id" #f)]
                      [nid  (hash-ref json "node_id" #f)])
                 (when (and id nid)
                   (make-remote-actor-ref id nid)))))
           peer-http-addrs)])
    (set! *peer-refs* refs)))

;; == Round-robin routing across self + peers ==

(def *peer-cursor* 0)
(def *peer-mutex* (make-mutex))

(def (route-to-peer-or-local! event local-ch)
  (if (null? *peer-refs*)
    (>!! local-ch event)
    (with-mutex *peer-mutex*
      (let* ([all (cons #f *peer-refs*)]   ;; #f = local
             [dest (list-ref all (modulo *peer-cursor* (length all)))])
        (set! *peer-cursor* (+ *peer-cursor* 1))
        (if (not dest)
          (>!! local-ch event)
          (guard (exn [#t (>!! local-ch event)])  ;; fallback to local
            (send dest (list 'work event))))))))
notes:
The peer's HTTP address (EDGE_PEERS) and transport address (node_id from /api/cluster/info) may differ. HTTP is for discovery; the node_id "host:port" is the TRANSPORT address used by make-remote-actor-ref.

connect-to-peers! MUST be called after the local HTTP server starts — otherwise peers calling back to discover our actor ID will get connection refused.

The remote actor on each peer receives (work event) messages and enqueues into its local ingest channel. The local actor behavior just does (>!! local-ch event).

actor-ref-id returns an exact integer that increments from 0 per process. It will be different on every restart. Always use the HTTP discovery flow; never hardcode actor IDs.

title: Run an in-process Raft consensus cluster for event ordering
code:
;; (std raft) is an in-process simulation using channels (no TCP).
;; Use it to demonstrate consensus, event ordering, and leader election
;; within one process. For cross-process Raft, bridge node inboxes
;; over (std actor transport) manually.

;; 1. Create N-node cluster (nodes wired together via in-memory channels)
(def cluster (make-raft-cluster 3))

;; 2. Start each node's message loop in a background thread
(for-each raft-start! (raft-cluster-nodes cluster))

;; 3. Wait for leader election (150–300ms election timeout, wait 500ms)
(sleep (make-time 'time-duration 500000000 0))

;; 4. Find the current leader
(def leader (raft-cluster-leader cluster))
(when leader
  (displayln "leader elected, term: " (raft-term leader)))

;; 5. Propose a command to the Raft log (fire-and-forget)
;;    Returns (values 'ok index) or (values 'not-leader #f)
(when leader
  (guard (exn [#t (void)])
    (raft-propose! leader '(event "evt_1" "payment.completed" 1776036929))))

;; 6. Inspect the committed log
(when leader
  (displayln "commit-index: " (raft-commit-index leader))
  (displayln "log: " (raft-log leader)))

;; 7. Check cluster state
(raft-leader? leader)          ;; => #t
(raft-state leader)            ;; => 'leader
(raft-term leader)             ;; => integer (increments on re-election)

;; 8. Graceful shutdown
(for-each raft-stop! (raft-cluster-nodes cluster))

;; Helper: fire-and-forget event logging (safe from any thread)
(def (raft-log-event! cluster event-id event-type)
  (let ([leader (raft-cluster-leader cluster)])
    (when leader
      (guard (exn [#t (void)])
        (raft-propose! leader
          `(event ,event-id ,event-type ,(time-second (current-time))))))))
notes:
(std raft) is IN-PROCESS ONLY. Uses (std misc channel) for node communication — no TCP. "Designed to be connected to a transport layer for cross-process operation."

(std crdt) does NOT exist in the stdlib — verified at runtime.

raft-node-id is NOT exported — you cannot get a node's integer ID from outside the module. Use raft-state/raft-term/raft-commit-index to inspect nodes.

Election timeout: random 150–300ms. Heartbeat: 50ms. A 500ms sleep after raft-start! is enough to reliably have a leader elected. The cluster may re-elect (term increments) due to timing — this is normal.

raft-propose! is synchronous (blocks until leader enqueues, replies ok/not-leader). For fire-and-forget, wrap in (guard (exn [#t (void)]) ...).

make-raft-cluster takes a COUNT (integer), not a list of nodes. Nodes get IDs 0..N-1.

title: Register and look up actors by name with (std actor registry)
code:
;; The registry is itself an actor — start it once before using register!/whereis.
(start-registry!)

;; Spawn an actor and register it under a name.
;; register! takes (name actor-ref) — NAME IS FIRST.
(def worker (spawn-actor (lambda (msg) (displayln "got: " msg))))
(register! 'my-worker worker)   ;; => 'ok or 'already-registered

;; Look up by name
(def ref (whereis 'my-worker))  ;; => actor-ref or #f
(when ref (send ref "hello"))

;; List all registered names
(registered-names)  ;; => list of symbols

;; Unregister explicitly (auto-unregistered on actor death via monitor)
(unregister! 'my-worker)

;; Note: registry uses ask-sync internally — calls block briefly.
;; The registry actor auto-unregisters names when their actors die
;; (via monitor DOWN messages).

;; IMPORTANT: The transport layer's lookup-local-actor uses numeric actor-ref-id,
;; NOT the registry. register!/whereis and transport are two separate lookup systems.
;; For cross-node refs, share actor-ref-id (integer) out-of-band, not the registry name.
notes:
register! arg order is (name actor-ref) — name FIRST. This is the opposite of what you might expect from Erlang's register/2 which takes (Name, Pid). Easy to swap accidentally.

start-registry! must be called before any register!/whereis/unregister!/registered-names. The registry is an actor — without it running, ask-sync will hang or error.

The registry and transport lookup are INDEPENDENT systems:
- registry: symbolic name → actor-ref (local process only)
- transport lookup-local-actor: numeric integer ID → actor-ref (used by remote send dispatch)

spawn-actor can optionally take a name: (spawn-actor behavior 'my-name) — this stores the name in actor-ref-name field but does NOT register it in the registry. You must call register! separately.

title: Fix "multiple definitions for atom? and meta" in compile-program (R6RS mode)
code:
;; PROBLEM: When compiling a .ss file with compile-program (R6RS top-level
;; program mode — used for WPO/static binary builds), you may get:
;;
;;   Exception: multiple definitions for meta and atom? in body
;;              (top-level-program ...)
;;
;; CAUSE: Chezscheme exports BOTH `meta` (a Chez syntax keyword) and
;; `atom?` as names. (jerboa prelude) re-exports its own versions of
;; both from (std misc meta) and (std misc atom). In --script mode
;; this is silently resolved by last-wins, but in R6RS top-level
;; program mode (compile-program) duplicate bindings are a fatal error.
;;
;; FIX: Exclude `atom?` and `meta` from chezscheme in the import clause.
;; This mirrors what the prelude itself does internally.

;; WRONG — works in --script but fails in compile-program:
#;(import (except (chezscheme)
            make-hash-table hash-table? sort sort! format printf fprintf
            iota 1+ 1- path-extension path-absolute?
            with-input-from-string with-output-to-string
            make-date partition)
          (jerboa prelude) ...)

;; CORRECT — add atom? and meta to the except clause:
(import (except (chezscheme)
          make-hash-table hash-table?
          sort sort! format printf fprintf
          iota 1+ 1-
          path-extension path-absolute?
          with-input-from-string with-output-to-string
          make-date partition
          atom? meta)       ; ← must add these for compile-program
        (except (jerboa prelude) make-time or-else)
        ...)

;; NOTE: `make-time` must also be excluded from the prelude (not chezscheme)
;; so that Chez's make-time remains available for (make-time 'time-duration ...).
;; `or-else` conflicts between (jerboa prelude) and (std stm).
notes:
The prelude's own internal import already does (except (chezscheme) ... atom? meta) — so in --script mode the prelude's versions win silently. In compile-program (R6RS top-level program) mode, having both chezscheme AND prelude export the same name is a fatal "multiple definitions in body" error even though the error message says "in body" (not "in imports"). The isolated test with an empty body + full imports will ALSO fail — the error is an import-level conflict despite the misleading "in body" message.

Additional names that may need to be excluded from chezscheme depending on your imports: `merge` (if importing (std ...) modules that export it). The full safe set to exclude from chezscheme when using (jerboa prelude): make-hash-table hash-table? sort sort! format printf fprintf iota 1+ 1- path-extension path-absolute? with-input-from-string with-output-to-string make-date partition make-time atom? meta.

title: Fix "undefined reference to _dl_find_object" when linking static binary with GCC 13 + musl
notes: This stub is needed when: (1) the build host has GCC 13+ installed and (2) you're using musl-gcc for static linking. The GCC 13 exception handler library references `_dl_find_object` which was added to glibc 2.35 for finding unwind tables in dynamically loaded libraries. Since musl has no such function and Chez Scheme doesn't use GCC-style stack unwinding, the stub returning -1 is safe. This stub must be in the same translation unit as main() to ensure it takes precedence over any weak symbol in the archives.

title: Auto-set JEMACS_STATIC=1 in static binary C main to skip load-shared-object
notes:
Two modules in the Jerboa stdlib that had hard (error ...) at the end of their load-shared-object chain and needed JEMACS_STATIC guards added: (std db sqlite-native) and (std os epoll-native). Modules like (std net tls-rustls) and (std crypto native-rust) already ended with #t and degraded gracefully. The check pattern used in (std net io) and added to others: `(let ([v (getenv "JEMACS_STATIC")]) (and v (not (string=? v "")) (not (string=? v "0"))))` — handles unset (v=#f), empty string, and "0" as all meaning "not static".

Note: (std net io) uses this check to skip `(load-shared-object #f)` — the `#f` form loads the current process's symbols via dlopen(NULL). In a static binary this is unnecessary since all symbols are pre-registered via Sforeign_symbol.

title: Correct libjerboa_native.a crypto symbol names for Sforeign_symbol registration
notes:
The musl-target libjerboa_native.a is pre-built in the Docker image. If you add new Rust FFI functions (like jerboa_eventfd_* was added), the archive may be stale. Rebuild inside Docker:

```bash
docker run --rm -v ~/mine/jerboa:/build/mine/jerboa -w /build/mine/jerboa/jerboa-native-rs \
  jerboa21/jerboa cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl --no-default-features
```

Note the `:ro` flag is removed for the rebuild run, then restored for the actual static build.

To check what's actually in the archive:
```bash
nm libjerboa_native.a | grep "^[0-9a-f].*T jerboa_" | awk '{print $3}' | sort
```

title: Enable Clojure reader compatibility mode (#!cloj / reader-cloj-mode)
code:
;; === Option 1: file directive (Jerboa reader only — REPL, jerboa-read-file) ===
;; Put at the top of any .ss file loaded through the Jerboa reader:
#!cloj
;; Now Clojure syntax is active for the rest of this file:
;;   {}        → hash-map literal     {:a 1 :b 2}
;;   #()       → anonymous function   #(+ % 1)  → (lambda (%1) (+ %1 1))
;;   :name     → keyword              :foo  → #:foo
;;   nil       → #f
;;   true/#f   → #t / #f
;;   @x        → (deref x)            (always-on, no mode needed)
;;   #{}       → eq-hashtable set     #{1 2 3}  (always-on)

;; === Option 2: programmatic activation ===
(import (jerboa cloj))
(activate-cloj-reader!)   ; sets (reader-cloj-mode #t) in current scope

;; === Option 3: use (jerboa clojure) — activates automatically ===
(import (jerboa clojure))
;; reader-cloj-mode, fn-literal, activate-cloj-reader! all re-exported

;; === fn-literal examples ===
;; #(+ % 1)          → (lambda (%1) (+ %1 1))
;; #(str %1 " " %2)  → (lambda (%1 %2) (str %1 " " %2))
;; #(apply + %&)     → (lambda %& (apply + %&))
;; Can also call fn-literal directly (without reader mode):
(import (jerboa cloj))
(def double (fn-literal (* %1 2)))  ; same as (lambda (%1) (* %1 2))
(double 5)  ; => 10

;; === Mode query ===
(reader-cloj-mode)       ; → #t or #f
(reader-cloj-mode #t)    ; activate
(reader-cloj-mode #f)    ; deactivate (restore Jerboa defaults)
notes:
IMPORTANT: Clojure reader syntax ({}  #()  :name  nil/true/false) ONLY works via the Jerboa reader pipeline (REPL, jerboa-read-file, jerboa-read-string). It does NOT work in files run with `scheme --script` or jerbuild-compiled .ss files — those use Chez's native (read port).

Syntax gated on (reader-cloj-mode):
  {}      — default=method dispatch {method obj args}, cloj=hash-map literal
  #()     — default=vector #(1 2 3), cloj=anonymous function #(+ % 1)
  :name   — default=module path :std/sort, cloj=keyword :foo
  nil/true/false — default=symbols, cloj=mapped to #f/#t/#f

Always-on (no mode needed):
  @x      — deref (clojure atom deref)
  #{}     — eq-hashtable set literal (hash-set items)

fn-literal: % is alias for %1. %& collects rest args. No nesting — #() inside #() is undefined.

Parameter scoping: (reader-cloj-mode #t) in a compiled library body does NOT persist to importing code. Use activate-cloj-reader! as an exported function that callers invoke, or rely on #!cloj at file top.

title: Correct sort argument order: (sort list comparator)
code:
;; Jerboa sort: LIST first, comparator second
(sort '(3 1 2) <)              ;; → (1 2 3)
(sort '("c" "a" "b") string<?) ;; → ("a" "b" "c")
(sort items (lambda (a b) (> (car a) (car b)))) ;; descending by car

;; WRONG (predicate first — causes "not a procedure" runtime error):
;; (sort < '(3 1 2))         ← DO NOT USE
notes: CLAUDE.md incorrectly claims (sort predicate list) is 'Chez arg order'. The actual Jerboa/Chez sort is (sort list comparator) — list FIRST, comparator SECOND. Passing predicate first causes a cryptic 'Exception in list-sort: ... is not a procedure' error because sort tries to call the list as a procedure.

title: Chez map applies lambda right-to-left — never use set! mutations inside map
code:
;; WRONG: Chez map applies lambda right-to-left when building the result list.
;; Counter increments from the rightmost element first, so indices are reversed.
(let ([i 0])
  (map (lambda (x)
         (let ([s (list x i)])
           (set! i (+ i 1))
           s))
       '(a b c)))
;; => ((a 2) (b 1) (c 0))   ← WRONG: c gets 0 not 2

;; CORRECT: use an explicit left-to-right tail-recursive loop
(let loop ([lst '(a b c)] [i 0] [acc '()])
  (if (null? lst)
      (reverse acc)
      (loop (cdr lst) (+ i 1)
            (cons (list (car lst) i) acc))))
;; => ((a 0) (b 1) (c 2))   ← correct
notes: Chez Scheme's (map f lst) internally recurses on the cdr before calling f on car, so side effects (set!) run from right to left even though the result list is in left-to-right order. This bit us hard in streaming aggregation slot index computation: agg-spec slots (agg count 0) (agg avg 1) were emitted as (agg count 1) (agg avg 0) because avg (rightmost) got ai=0. Fix: always use an explicit loop with acc+reverse when you need left-to-right evaluation order with counters.

title: Export jerboa-db datoms to DuckDB for OLAP queries
code:
;; Export current db snapshot to an in-memory DuckDB engine
(def conn (connect ":memory:"))
;; ... transact schema and data ...

;; Create analytics engine from current db snapshot
(def adb (analytics-export! (db conn)))

;; Run SQL against two tables: datoms(e,a,a_name,v_long,v_double,v_string,v_bool,v_ref,v_instant,tx,added)
;;                             and attrs(id,ident,value_type,cardinality)
(def rows (analytics-query adb "SELECT COUNT(*) as cnt FROM datoms"))
(display (cdr (assoc "cnt" (car rows)))) ;; => integer count

;; Self-join to compute per-department average age
(def avg-rows (analytics-query adb
  "SELECT dept.v_string as dept, AVG(age.v_long) as avg_age
   FROM datoms dept JOIN datoms age ON dept.e = age.e
   WHERE dept.a_name = 'person/dept' AND age.a_name = 'person/age'
     AND dept.added = TRUE AND age.added = TRUE
   GROUP BY dept.v_string ORDER BY dept.v_string"))

;; Re-sync after more transactions
(transact! conn '(...))
(analytics-sync! adb (db conn))  ;; full refresh (truncate + reload)

;; Close when done
(analytics-close! adb)
notes: analytics-export! = new-analytics-engine + analytics-sync! in one call. Returns an analytics-engine. analytics-query returns list of alists (column-name . value). The 'added' boolean column distinguishes assertions from retractions — always filter WHERE added = TRUE for current values. DuckDB loaded lazily so the native library (libjerboa_native.so with duckdb feature) is only required when analytics functions are first called, not at import time. Requires libjerboa_native.so built with 'duckdb' feature (add duckdb = { version = \"1\", features = [\"bundled\"] } to Cargo.toml and mod duckdb_native in lib.rs, then make native in jerboa repo).

title: Normalize #\escape and #!void before feeding source to Chez csv10 reader
code:
;; PROBLEM: Docker Chez Scheme csv10 rejects two token forms even with
;; #!chezscheme mode prefix:
;;   #\escape  → "invalid character name"  (use #\esc instead)
;;   #!void    → "invalid syntax #!void"   (use (void) instead)
;;
;; SOLUTION: Text-preprocess the source string before handing it to
;; (with-input-from-string src read) in jerbuild.

(def (normalize-chez-reader-tokens src)
  ;; Replace #!void with (void) — exact token, word boundary via space/paren
  (let* ([s (re-replace-all (re "#!void") src "(void)")]
         ;; Replace #\escape with #\esc
         [s (re-replace-all (re "#\\\\escape") s "#\\esc")])
    s))

;; Usage in jerbuild's read loop:
(def (read-forms-from-string src)
  (let ([normalized (normalize-chez-reader-tokens src)])
    (with-input-from-string normalized
      (lambda ()
        (let loop ([forms '()])
          (let ([form (read)])
            (if (eof-object? form)
                (reverse forms)
                (loop (cons form forms)))))))))
notes: This only affects jerbuild's .ss→.sls transpiler pipeline when targeting Chez csv10 (the Docker build). Interactive Chez sessions and newer Chez releases may accept #\escape. The preprocess step must happen on the raw source string, BEFORE calling read — you cannot fix this at the AST level because the reader fails before producing an AST. Apply normalization once per source file, not per form.

title: Handle Gerbil stdlib modules missing from Jerboa via a drop-imports list in jerbuild
code:
;; PROBLEM: Gerbil .ss files may import modules that don't exist in the Jerboa
;; distribution. Known absent modules (as of jerboa-shell/compartment):
;;
;;   (std os signal-handler)  → add-signal-handler!/remove-signal-handler! are
;;                               in (std os signal) instead
;;   (std misc hash)          → hash operations come from (jerboa core) / prelude
;;
;; SOLUTION: Maintain a *drop-imports* alist in jerbuild mapping a missing module
;; to either #f (drop entirely) or a replacement module path.

(def *drop-imports*
  '(;; Module path as symbol list      → replacement or #f
    ((std os signal-handler)           . (std os signal))
    ((std misc hash)                   . #f)        ;; provided by (jerboa core)
  ))

(def (translate-import imp)
  (let ([entry (assoc imp *drop-imports*)])
    (cond
      [(not entry) imp]                             ;; keep as-is
      [(cdr entry) => (lambda (rep) rep)]           ;; replace
      [else #f])))                                  ;; drop (filter out #f)

;; In jerbuild's import-rewriting pass:
(def (rewrite-imports imports)
  (filter-map translate-import imports))
notes: The drop/replace list is distribution-specific — update it whenever porting new Gerbil modules. If a symbol from the dropped module is actually used, you'll get an unbound-variable error at compile time, which is better than a module-not-found error at load time. For (std misc hash), the symbols (hash-ref, hash-put!, etc.) are already in (jerboa core) so no replacement import is needed.

title: Add inter-library conflict rules in jerbuild when (jerboa core) re-exports from co-imported modules
code:
;; PROBLEM: jerbuild auto-imports (jerboa core) into every generated .sls file.
;; (jerboa core) re-exports symbols from several std modules, including:
;;   - string-index  (from (std misc string) and (jsh util))
;;   - partition     (and others from (std misc list))
;;   - path-normalize (from (std misc path))
;;   - error-message  (from (std error))
;;
;; If the .ss file also explicitly imports (std misc string), (jsh util), etc.,
;; the generated .sls gets duplicate export errors.
;;
;; SOLUTION: Define conflict rules as an alist of (co-import → symbols-to-exclude-from-jerboa-core).
;; When generating the import clause for (jerboa core), wrap it in (except ...) for
;; any conflicting symbols detected in the file's import list.

(def *jerboa-core-conflict-rules*
  ;; (co-imported-module . (symbols exported by BOTH (jerboa core) AND co-import))
  '(((std misc string)  . (string-index string-pad-right string-pad-left))
    ((jsh util)         . (string-index))
    ((std misc list)    . (partition append-map filter-map))
    ((std misc path)    . (path-normalize))
    ((std error)        . (error-message))))

(def (compute-jerboa-core-excepts file-imports)
  ;; Collect all symbols to exclude from (jerboa core) given what else is imported
  (apply append
    (filter-map
      (lambda (rule)
        (and (member (car rule) file-imports)
             (cdr rule)))
      *jerboa-core-conflict-rules*)))

(def (make-jerboa-core-import file-imports)
  (let ([excepts (compute-jerboa-core-excepts file-imports)])
    (if (null? excepts)
        '(jerboa core)
        `(except (jerboa core) ,@excepts))))

;; Usage in jerbuild's .sls generation:
;; (make-jerboa-core-import '((std misc string) (std net request)))
;; → (except (jerboa core) string-index string-pad-right string-pad-left)
notes: The conflict rules list must be kept in sync with (jerboa core)'s actual exports. Run `jerboa_module_exports (jerboa core)` to get the current full list if you suspect new conflicts. The approach of excluding from (jerboa core) (rather than from the specific module) is preferred because (jerboa core) is auto-injected and harder to control, while the explicit module import is explicit and expected. Add new rules when you see "multiple definitions for X" during a jerbuild-generated compile.

title: Guard jerbuild's wrap-mutable-exports against set! reassignment of defined functions
code:
;; PROBLEM: jerbuild's wrap-mutable-exports pass wraps every exported variable
;; in a (define-syntax name (identifier-syntax (get-mutable-var 'name))) form
;; to make it mutable across library boundaries.
;;
;; When a module body contains BOTH:
;;   (def (fn args...) body)       ;; defines fn as a function
;;   (set! fn other-fn)            ;; reassigns fn to another function
;;
;; jerbuild incorrectly generates (define-syntax fn ...) as a wrapper AND
;; also keeps the original (define fn ...) — producing "duplicate definition for fn".
;;
;; SOLUTION: Before wrapping a name, check whether it appears as a (set! name ...)
;; target in the body. If so, it is already handled as a mutable cell and must NOT
;; get an additional define-syntax wrapper.

(def (set!-targets body-forms)
  ;; Collect all names that appear as (set! name ...) in the top-level body
  (filter-map
    (lambda (form)
      (match form
        (('set! name _) name)
        (_ #f)))
    body-forms))

(def (wrap-mutable-exports exports body-forms locally-function-vars)
  ;; Don't wrap names that are either locally defined as functions (they get
  ;; a proper (define ...) in the output) or are set! targets (they are
  ;; already mutable cells and re-wrapping causes duplicate definition).
  (let ([skip (append locally-function-vars (set!-targets body-forms))])
    (filter-map
      (lambda (name)
        (if (memq name skip)
            #f
            `(define-syntax ,name
               (identifier-syntax (get-mutable-var ',name)))))
      exports)))
notes: The root cause is that Gerbil allows (set! fn other-fn) to reassign module-level functions freely; in the R6RS .sls output, jerbuild models this with mutable cells. But when fn is also locally defined (def (fn ...) ...), adding a define-syntax wrapper on top is a duplicate. The fix requires consulting both locally-function-vars (already tracked) and set!-targets (newly tracked) before wrapping. This bug only surfaces when a module uses set! to reassign a named function, not for reassigning plain variables.

title: Use root-level .ss overrides instead of build-jerboa.ss patches for missing functionality
code:
;; PROBLEM: The old build pipeline (build-jerboa.ss) patched generated .sls files
;; after transpilation — e.g., injecting recorder-ensure-record-key! into recorder.sls,
;; or adding extra define forms. In the new jerbuild flow, these patches NEVER run.
;; Functions added only via build-jerboa.ss patches are silently absent.
;;
;; SOLUTION: Instead of patching, create a root-level .ss override file.
;; jerbuild resolves source files in this priority order:
;;   1. ./name.ss   (project root)   ← takes priority
;;   2. gerbil-shell/name.ss         ← upstream submodule fallback
;;
;; To add missing functionality to e.g. recorder.ss without touching the submodule:

;; Step 1: Check if a root override already exists
;;   ls ./recorder.ss 2>/dev/null || echo "falls back to gerbil-shell/recorder.ss"

;; Step 2: If not, copy from submodule and update imports
;;   cp gerbil-shell/recorder.ss ./recorder.ss
;;   # Change :gsh/ imports to :jsh/

;; Step 3: Add the missing function directly in the .ss source
(def (recorder-ensure-record-key! rec key)
  ;; ... implementation ...
  (unless (hash-key? (recorder-data rec) key)
    (hash-put! (recorder-data rec) key '())))

;; Step 4: Build — jerbuild will pick up ./recorder.ss automatically.
;;
;; NEVER edit src/jsh/*.sls directly — they are overwritten on every build.
notes: The shell/ directory (shell/recorder.ss etc.) is NOT used by the build — it contains dead copies. Only ./name.ss (root) and gerbil-shell/name.ss (submodule) matter. When copying from the submodule, change all import paths from :gsh/foo to :jsh/foo. If the missing function was injected by build-jerboa.ss via a string-contains/string-append patch, you need to read build-jerboa.ss carefully to understand what was being injected — it is usually a define or a set! — and replicate it properly in the .ss source.

title: Start a 2-node TCP Raft cluster with staged peer registration
code:
;; Staged setup: port numbers are OS-assigned, so we can't know them until after start.
;; 1. Start node-a with no peers (port 0 = OS-assigned).
(define-values (tnode-a rconn-a)
  (start-transport-db-node! 'node-a '() ":memory:" 0))
(define port-a (transport-node-listen-port tnode-a))

;; 2. Start node-b knowing node-a's port.
(define-values (tnode-b rconn-b)
  (start-transport-db-node! 'node-b `((node-a "127.0.0.1" ,port-a)) ":memory:" 0))
(define port-b (transport-node-listen-port tnode-b))

;; 3. Tell node-a about node-b now that we know its port.
;;    Use transport-node-add-peer! (not raft-node-peers-set!) — it is thread-safe
;;    and also updates next-index/match-index if the node is already a leader.
(transport-node-add-peer! tnode-a 'node-b "127.0.0.1" port-b)

;; 4. Wait for leader election (~150–300ms).
(sleep (make-time 'time-duration 500000000 0))

;; 5. Identify leader.
(define leader   (if (cluster-leader? rconn-a) rconn-a rconn-b))
(define follower (if (cluster-leader? rconn-a) rconn-b rconn-a))

;; 6. Transact through leader (use alist format — NOT curly braces in quoted lists).
(cluster-transact! leader
  (list '((db/ident . person/name)
          (db/valueType . db.type/string)
          (db/cardinality . db.cardinality/one))))

;; 7. Query from either node.
(q '[(find ?n) (where (?e person/name ?n))] (cluster-db leader))

;; 8. Stop both nodes.
(stop-transport-node! tnode-a)
(stop-transport-node! tnode-b)
notes:
CRITICAL: Transaction data must use alist format ((attr . val) ...), NOT {attr val} curly-brace syntax inside quoted lists. The Jerboa reader expands {f obj args} to (~ obj 'f args) before quoting, producing method-call forms instead of entity maps. Use (list '((attr . val))) instead of '({attr val}).

transport-node-add-peer! uses raft-node-add-peer! which is thread-safe and also updates next-index/match-index on leader nodes, preventing send-heartbeats! from crashing with (cdr #f) on the new peer.

The apply fiber polls every 50ms. For follower replication queries, add a short wait or use wait-until polling.

title: Chez (random n) is not thread-safe — add node-specific jitter for concurrent timers
code:
;; PROBLEM: Chez Scheme's (random n) is NOT thread-safe.
;; Two threads that call (random n) concurrently can read the same PRNG state
;; and return identical values — even with large n.
;;
;; This causes ~50% failure rate when two nodes start simultaneously and both
;; compute election timeouts from (+ 150 (random 150)):
;;   Thread A: (random 150) → 73   → timeout = 223ms
;;   Thread B: (random 150) → 73   → timeout = 223ms  (same! → split vote every time)
;;
;; FIX: Add a deterministic, identity-derived constant offset so different nodes
;; always get different base timeouts regardless of PRNG state:

(define (node-id-hash id)
  (cond
    [(integer? id) id]
    [(symbol? id)
     (let* ([s (symbol->string id)] [n (string-length s)])
       (let loop ([i 0] [h 0])
         (if (= i n) h
             (loop (+ i 1) (+ (* h 31) (char->integer (string-ref s i)))))))]
    [else 0]))

;; Usage: each node gets a unique base, random adds further jitter
(define (election-timeout-ms node-id)
  ;; 150ms base + 0-74ms id-hash + 0-74ms random = 150-298ms total
  ;; Guaranteed non-equal for different node-ids even if random returns same value
  (+ 150
     (modulo (node-id-hash node-id) 75)
     (random 75)))
notes:
This pattern is critical for any distributed algorithm where multiple nodes start simultaneously and need randomized timeouts that must not collide. Without it, 2-node Raft clusters exhibit ~50% election failure rate.

The fix works because the id-hash component is a compile-time constant per node — no PRNG involved — so even if (random 75) returns the same value for both threads, the total timeouts differ by the hash offset.

For truly thread-safe random, you could use a per-thread PRNG seeded with the thread ID, but the hash-offset approach is simpler and sufficient for most distributed timer use cases.

title: Apply Raft committed entries to a DB connection via replication-for-each-committed!
code:
;; For TCP/multi-process clusters, each node applies from its OWN raft-node log.
;; (Contrast with in-process clusters which read the LEADER's log directly.)
;;
;; replication-for-each-committed! iterates newly committed entries beyond
;; last-applied-index and calls (proc entry-index tx-ops) for each tx entry.
;; It also advances the watermark (last-applied-index) before each call —
;; at-most-once semantics so a crash mid-apply still moves forward.

(define (start-apply-fiber! conn state)
  (fork-thread
    (lambda ()
      (let loop ()
        (sleep (chez-make-time 'time-duration 50000000 0)) ;; 50ms poll
        (when (replication-running? state)
          (replication-for-each-committed! state
            (lambda (entry-index tx-ops)
              (guard (exn [#t
                           (display
                             (string-append "apply: skip tx at index "
                                            (number->string entry-index) "\n"))])
                (transact! conn tx-ops))))
          (loop))))))

;; NOTE: This requires the (std raft) AppendEntries bug fix:
;; The follower's log handler must use (<= entry-index prev-idx) when keeping
;; existing entries — NOT (< entry-index prev-idx). The < version drops the
;; verified pivot entry on every RPC, leaving followers with only the single
;; most-recent log entry and causing "Unknown attribute in pattern" errors
;; when earlier schema transactions are missing from the replay.
notes:
Use this pattern (per-node log) for TCP/multi-process clusters where you cannot read the leader's raft-node object directly.

For in-process clusters (start-local-db-cluster), use apply-committed-from-cluster! which reads the LEADER's log directly — this avoids the (std raft) log-truncation bug without needing the <= fix, and is the approach used in cluster.ss.

The guard around (transact! conn tx-ops) is essential: a failed tx (e.g. schema violation, serialization error) must not abort the apply fiber. The watermark has already advanced, so the failed entry will not be retried.

title: Transducer-Fusing Smart Threading with (std injest)
code:
(import (jerboa prelude))
(import (std injest))

;; => is smart thread-last: fuses adjacent recognised steps into a
;; single transducer pipeline, falls back to plain ->> for others.
;; Recognised heads: map filter remove take drop mapcat append-map
;;   take-while drop-while filter-map dedupe deduplicate
;;   indexing enumerate indexed

;; Two consecutive recognised steps → fused (no intermediate list)
(=> '(1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9)
    (filter odd?)
    (map (lambda (x) (* x x))))
;; => (1 9 25 49 81)

;; Mix recognised + unrecognised: unrecognised steps are plain thread-last
(=> '(1 2 3 4 5)
    (filter odd?)     ;; recognised — fused into transducer run
    (map 1+)          ;; recognised — fused into same run
    length)           ;; unrecognised — emitted as (length <prev>)
;; => 3

;; Single recognised step: emitted as native call (no transducer overhead)
(=> '(1 2 3 4 5) (filter odd?))  ;; expands to (filter odd? '(1 2 3 4 5))
;; => (1 3 5)

;; x>> is strict: ALL steps must be transducer-convertible (compile-time error otherwise)
(x>> '(1 2 3 4 5 6)
     (filter even?)
     (map (lambda (x) (* x 10)))
     (take 3))
;; => (20 40 60)

;; x>> with a non-transducer step causes a syntax error at expand time:
;; (x>> '(1 2 3) (filter odd?) length)  ;; ERROR: 'length' not recognised

;; With (enumerate/indexing), pairs each element with its index:
(=> '(a b c) (enumerate))
;; => ((0 . a) (1 . b) (2 . c))
notes:
CRITICAL GOTCHA: (std injest) does (import (except (chezscheme) =>)) internally, which means importing it shadows Chez Scheme's cond => arrow form. You cannot use (cond [test => proc]) if (std injest) is imported in the same module.

=> is NOT in (jerboa prelude) — it requires (import (std injest)).

Fusion only kicks in for runs of 2+ recognised steps. A single recognised step is emitted as a plain call to avoid transducer allocation overhead.

Depends on (std transducer) — imported automatically by (std injest), no separate import needed.

Landed: 2026-04-21.

title: Use persistent maps/sets/vectors as hashtable keys
code:
;; pmap / pvec / pset participate in Chez's equal? / equal-hash
;; protocols via record-type-equal-procedure and
;; record-type-hash-procedure — so two structurally-equal persistent
;; collections compare equal? and hash to the same bucket. That makes
;; them usable as keys in (make-hashtable equal-hash equal?) without
;; any wrapping or manual hashing.
;;
;; An eq-hashtable still works but keys by identity (pointer compare)
;; — two pmaps with the same entries would live in different buckets.
;; Use equal-hashtable when you want value-semantics.

(define ht (make-hashtable equal-hash equal?))

;; Insert with a persistent-map key.
(hashtable-set! ht (make-persistent-map 'a 1 'b 2) 'first)

;; A freshly-constructed structurally-equal map hits the same bucket.
(hashtable-ref ht (make-persistent-map 'b 2 'a 1) #f)
;; => first   (key order didn't matter; pmap=? ignores iteration order)

;; Same pattern for pvec and pset:
(define seen (make-hashtable equal-hash equal?))
(hashtable-set! seen (persistent-vector 1 2 3) 'row-a)
(hashtable-ref seen (persistent-vector 1 2 3) #f)
;; => row-a

(define tag-groups (make-hashtable equal-hash equal?))
(hashtable-set! tag-groups (make-persistent-set 'red 'blue) '(item-1 item-5))
(hashtable-ref tag-groups (make-persistent-set 'blue 'red) #f)
;; => (item-1 item-5)

;; Hash function note: for large pmaps the equal-hash computation
;; walks every entry. If keys are hot, wrap the pmap in a struct
;; that caches its hash on first compute.
notes: Enabled in Jerboa by Round 4 Phase 25 (2026-04-23) — record-type-equal-procedure / record-type-hash-procedure wiring on pmap/pvec/pset. Before that, persistent collections hashed by identity, so two structurally-equal maps would live in different hashtable buckets. Use eq-hashtable when you want identity-keyed, equal-hashtable when you want value-keyed.

title: Destructure persistent maps/vectors/sets in match patterns
code:
;; (std match2) has pattern arms for all three persistent collection
;; types — alternating key/value for pmap, positional for pvec, and
;; membership-only for pset.  Aliases: pmap/persistent-map/imap,
;; pvec/persistent-vector/ivec, pset/persistent-set.
;;
;; Non-matching values (wrong type, missing key, wrong length) fall
;; through to the next clause — no exception — so these patterns
;; compose freely with `_` and other match arms.

;; --- pmap: alternating key pat key pat ... ---
(match (make-persistent-map 'name "alice" 'age 30)
  [(pmap 'name n 'age a) (list n a)]
  [_ 'no-match])
;; => ("alice" 30)

;; Keys are evaluated as expressions (usually quoted). A missing key
;; falls through; the map can have extra keys not listed in the pattern.
(match (make-persistent-map 'a 1 'b 2 'c 3)
  [(pmap 'missing x) x]   ;; falls through — 'missing not in map
  [(pmap 'a v)        v]) ;; matches
;; => 1

;; --- pvec: exact positional match ---
(match (persistent-vector 10 20 30)
  [(pvec a b c) (+ a b c)]
  [_ 'wrong-shape])
;; => 60

;; Length must be exact (rest-pattern not yet supported).
(match (persistent-vector 1 2 3)
  [(pvec a b) 'two]
  [(pvec a b c) 'three])
;; => three

;; --- pset: membership check, superset allowed ---
(match (make-persistent-set 'red 'blue 'green)
  [(pset 'red 'blue) 'has-both]
  [_ 'not-both])
;; => has-both

;; --- Nesting — the patterns compose with each other and with
;;     standard match constructs like and, or, guards, predicates. ---
(match (make-persistent-map 'user (make-persistent-map 'id 42 'name "bob"))
  [(pmap 'user (pmap 'id (and (? number?) uid) 'name n))
   (list uid n)])
;; => (42 "bob")

;; Guards work as usual with (where …).
(match (make-persistent-map 'age 30)
  [(pmap 'age a) (where (>= a 18)) 'adult]
  [(pmap 'age a) 'minor])
;; => adult
notes: Added in Jerboa by Round 4 Phase 27 (2026-04-23). Before that, match had no direct support for persistent collections — users had to manually predicate-check and ref. Each arm emits a persistent-*? type-guard around the whole has?/ref chain so non-matching values fall through cleanly (a list won't crash against a pmap pattern, for example). Predicate subpattern note: prefer (and (? pred?) name) over (? pred? -> name); the latter binds the bool result of (pred? v), not v itself.

title: Iterate persistent maps/vectors/sets with for / for/collect / for/fold
code:
;; (std iter) has fused for / for/collect / for/fold arms for six
;; persistent-collection iterators:
;;
;;   in-pvec         → elements in index order
;;   in-pmap         → (cons k v) pairs  (alias of in-pmap-pairs)
;;   in-pmap-pairs   → (cons k v) pairs
;;   in-pmap-keys    → keys only
;;   in-pmap-values  → values only
;;   in-pset         → elements
;;
;; The fused arms iterate the HAMT / trie directly — no intermediate
;; list is materialised.  Before Phase 28 these went through a
;; fallback that consed an entire list up front.
;;
;; Iteration order for pmap / pset is hash-layout-dependent, not
;; insertion-order.  pvec iterates in index order.

;; --- pvec: index-loop fusion, O(1) allocation ---
(for ((x (in-pvec (persistent-vector 10 20 30))))
  (displayln x))
;; prints 10 20 30

(for/collect ((x (in-pvec (persistent-vector 1 2 3 4 5))))
  (* x x))
;; => (1 4 9 16 25)

(for/fold ((sum 0))
          ((x (in-pvec (persistent-vector 1 2 3 4 5))))
  (+ sum x))
;; => 15

;; --- pmap: iterate pairs, walk HAMT directly ---
(for/fold ((s 0))
          ((kv (in-pmap (make-persistent-map 'a 1 'b 2 'c 3))))
  (+ s (cdr kv)))
;; => 6

;; When you only need keys or values, use the specialised iterator —
;; it avoids allocating the pair cons cell.
(for/collect ((k (in-pmap-keys (make-persistent-map 'x 1 'y 2))))
  k)
;; => some permutation of (x y)  — hash order, not insertion order

(for/fold ((s 0))
          ((v (in-pmap-values (make-persistent-map 'a 10 'b 20 'c 30))))
  (+ s v))
;; => 60

;; --- pset: iterate elements ---
(for/collect ((x (in-pset (make-persistent-set 1 2 3)))) x)
;; => some permutation of (1 2 3)

;; --- Works with the clause keywords too ---
(for/collect ((kv (in-pmap (make-persistent-map 'a 1 'b 2 'c 3 'd 4)))
              when: (even? (cdr kv)))
  (car kv))
;; => keys whose values are even, in hash-iteration order
notes: Fused arms added in Jerboa by Round 4 Phase 28 (2026-04-23). Before that, (for [x (in-pmap m)] ...) first materialised the entire map into a list of pairs, then walked the list — expensive for large maps. Now the macro expands into a direct persistent-map-for-each call. When you need insertion-ordered iteration over a pmap/pset, this is not what you want — pmap/pset are HAMT-backed and iterate in hash-bucket order. Use a pvec or ordered-map if ordering matters.

title: Install atom validators with pre-check semantics
code:
;; set-validator! re-runs the validator against the CURRENT atom value
;; at install time. If the validator rejects or throws, installation
;; fails and the atom retains its old validator. This mirrors Clojure's
;; guarantee that a live atom never holds a value its validator rejects.
(define a (atom 1))
(set-validator! a (lambda (n) (and (integer? n) (positive? n))))

;; The validator runs inside the lock for swap!/update!/CAS (the new
;; value must validate before the store is committed) and OUTSIDE the
;; lock for reset! (reset! assigns under the lock but validates before
;; taking it — avoids re-entrance if the validator touches the atom).

;; Installing a stricter validator that doesn't hold on the current
;; value raises immediately — atoms never transition into an invalid
;; resting state:
(guard (_ [else (displayln "pre-check rejected")])
  (set-validator! a (lambda (n) (> n 100))))

;; Use get-validator to inspect; pass #f to set-validator! to remove.
(get-validator a)              ;; => the currently-installed procedure
(set-validator! a #f)          ;; => removes any validator
notes: The atom's validator lives inline in the record (not via an external registry). Pre-check semantics mean installation can fail — always wrap with `guard` when installing after construction. Validator exceptions are treated as rejections.

title: Multimethod hierarchies with derive + prefer-method
code:
;; Hierarchies let dispatch fall back through an isa? graph when no exact
;; method matches. Two API shapes: bare (mutates `global-hierarchy`) or
;; explicit (takes a hierarchy record from `make-hierarchy`).

;; 1. Bare form: uses the shared global-hierarchy.
(derive 'corgi 'dog)
(derive 'dog 'mammal)

(defmulti legs (lambda (animal) animal))
(defmethod legs 'mammal (_) 4)
(defmethod legs 'bird   (_) 2)

(legs 'corgi)    ;; => 4  (corgi -> dog -> mammal)

;; 2. Isolated form: each test fixture gets its own hierarchy.
(define h (make-hierarchy))
(derive h 'puppy 'dog)
(ancestors h 'puppy)           ;; => (dog)
(descendants h 'dog)           ;; => (puppy)
(isa? h 'puppy 'dog)           ;; => #t

;; Cycle detection: self-derives and indirect cycles raise.
(guard (_ [else 'cycle-rejected])
  (derive h 'a 'a))

;; Ambiguity: a dispatch value isa? multiple methods. prefer-method
;; breaks the tie.
(defmulti describe (lambda (x) x))
(defmethod describe 'swimmer (_) "swims")
(defmethod describe 'flyer   (_) "flies")
(derive 'duck 'swimmer)
(derive 'duck 'flyer)

(guard (_ [else 'ambiguous]) (describe 'duck))   ;; => 'ambiguous
(prefer-method describe 'swimmer 'flyer)
(describe 'duck)                                 ;; => "swims"

(preferred-methods describe)    ;; => ((swimmer . flyer))
notes: Exact dispatch wins over hierarchy. If ambiguity cannot be resolved by prefer-method, dispatch raises. Preferences live on the multimethod record, not the hierarchy — each defmulti has its own preference list.

title: Agent error modes, handlers, and timed await
code:
(def a (agent 0))

;; By default, agents latch the first error: subsequent sends raise
;; until restart-agent clears the error state ('fail mode).
(agent-error-mode a)           ;; => 'fail

;; 'continue mode swallows action errors so the worker keeps draining
;; its queue. Pair with set-error-handler! to keep observability.
(set-error-mode! a 'continue)
(set-error-handler! a
  (lambda (ag exn)
    (printf "agent ~s failed: ~a~%"
      ag (if (message-condition? exn) (condition-message exn) exn))))

(send a + 1)                                 ;; -> 1
(send a (lambda (_) (error 'boom "nope")))   ;; swallowed, handler runs
(send a + 10)                                ;; -> 11
(await a)
(agent-value a)                              ;; => 11
(agent-error a)                              ;; => #f  (not latched)

;; Timed await — returns #t if the queue drained in time, #f on timeout.
(send a (lambda (v)
          (sleep (make-time 'time-duration 0 1))  ;; 1 second
          (+ v 1)))
(await-for 50 a)                             ;; => #f  (50ms too short)
(await-for 2000 a)                           ;; => #t

;; Handler exceptions are swallowed — a broken handler can't crash
;; the agent worker.
(set-error-handler! a #f)                    ;; clear handler
(shutdown-agent! a)
notes: await-for polls for a marker action in 5ms ticks. Works in both fiber and OS-thread modes. set-error-mode! accepts only 'fail or 'continue; set-error-handler! accepts a procedure or #f.

title: Guard side-effecting code with io! inside dosync
code:
;; STM transactions may retry. Any side effect inside a transaction
;; body would be replayed — logs would double-print, network calls
;; would fire repeatedly. io! guards a body so it raises if called
;; inside a dosync/atomically, and runs unguarded elsewhere.

(define balance (make-ref 100))

(define (deposit! r amount)
  (dosync (alter r + amount)))

(define (audit-deposit! r amount)
  (dosync
    (alter r + amount)
    ;; Logging is a side effect — MUST NOT happen inside the txn.
    (io! (printf "deposited ~s~%" amount))))   ;; raises at runtime

(deposit! balance 50)
(ref-deref balance)                ;; => 150

(guard (_ [else 'caught-io])
  (audit-deposit! balance 25))     ;; => 'caught-io (txn rolled back)

(ref-deref balance)                ;; => 150  (unchanged)

;; Correct pattern: do the mutation in dosync, then the I/O outside.
(define (safe-deposit! r amount)
  (let ([new (dosync (alter r + amount))])
    (io! (printf "deposited ~s (now ~s)~%" amount new))
    new))

(safe-deposit! balance 25)         ;; prints; balance => 175
notes: io! is a macro that checks the *current-tx* parameter at runtime. Outside a transaction it's a pass-through. Nested dosync also triggers the raise.

title: Validate function args with std/spec in --script context (s-instrument workaround)
code:
;; `(s-instrument 'name)` uses set-top-level-value! to rebind a procedure.
;; In a `scheme --script file.ss` run, `def` creates a lexical binding
;; in the program body — set-top-level-value! cannot rewrite it, so
;; s-instrument silently no-ops and callers run the uninstrumented
;; function. Workaround: wrap the function body with an explicit
;; s-valid? / s-explain-str guard that works in any execution mode.

(s-def ::isbn  (s-and string? (s-pred (lambda (s) (> (string-length s) 10)))))
(s-def ::qty   (s-and integer? (s-pred positive?)))
;; Note: s-cat takes alternating tag+spec pairs, not a bare list of specs.
(s-def ::order (s-cat ':isbn '::isbn ':qty '::qty))

(def (place-order isbn qty)
  (unless (s-valid? '::order (list isbn qty))
    (error 'place-order "args do not conform"
           (s-explain-str '::order (list isbn qty))))
  (list 'order isbn qty))

(place-order "978-0553560732" 2)       ;; => (order "978-..." 2)
;; (place-order "short" -1)           ;; raises with a readable explanation
notes:
Two gotchas in one recipe: (1) s-instrument is effectively a REPL-only feature — plain Chez top-level-value is not the lexical env of a --script program, so the wrapper is installed on a name nobody calls. (2) s-cat's signature is `(s-cat :tag1 spec1 :tag2 spec2 ...)`, not `(s-cat spec1 spec2)`. The error message when you flatten the pairs reports the second argument as the validator for the first position, which is confusing. Always interleave.</notes>
</invoke>

title: Make (std multi) defmulti respect a derive hierarchy
code:
;; (std multi) exports derive/ancestors/isa? (§35) but defmulti's
;; dispatch does NOT consult a hierarchy automatically — it matches
;; the dispatch value exactly, then falls through to 'default. To get
;; Clojure-style isa?-aware dispatch, resolve the most specific
;; ancestor with a registered method inside the dispatch function.

(def events-h (make-hierarchy))
(derive events-h 'order/new-member-order 'order/any)
(derive events-h 'order/returning-order  'order/any)
(derive events-h 'order/any              'event/any)

(defmulti handle-event
  (lambda (evt)
    (let ([t (cdr (assq 'type evt))])
      (or (find (lambda (tag) (get-method handle-event tag))
                (cons t (ancestors events-h t)))
          t))))

(defmethod handle-event 'order/new-member-order (e)
  (str "welcome-discount for " (cdr (assq 'id e))))
(defmethod handle-event 'order/any (e)
  (str "logged order "   (cdr (assq 'id e))))
(defmethod handle-event 'event/any (e)
  (str "generic audit: " (cdr (assq 'type e))))

(handle-event '((type . order/returning-order) (id . 1002)))
;; => "logged order 1002" — dispatched via derive chain
notes:
Without this pattern, `(handle-event '((type . order/returning-order) ...))` raises "no method for dispatch value" because only the exact tag is looked up. `get-method` plus `ancestors` gives you walk-up-the-chain dispatch. The prelude's own `defmethod` shadows `std/multi`'s, so the `except` import on prelude is mandatory.</notes>
</invoke>

title: Sleep for N milliseconds with sleep-ms (prelude)
code:
;; sleep-ms is re-exported from both (jerboa prelude) and (std prelude).
;; No need to import Chez's make-time (which is shadowed by the prelude
;; with a date-style constructor).
(import (jerboa prelude))

(displayln "sleeping 150ms ...")
(sleep-ms 150)
(displayln "done")

;; Zero ms is a valid no-op.
(sleep-ms 0)

;; Non-negative integer only; negative or non-integer args raise.
(guard (_ [else (displayln "rejected as expected")])
  (sleep-ms -1))
notes: Prefer sleep-ms over `(sleep (make-time 'time-duration ns sec))`. The prelude shadows Chez's make-time with a date-style `(make-time h m s)` constructor, so reaching for the low-level time-duration form requires renaming the import — sleep-ms encapsulates that dance. Supersedes the older thread-sleep-prelude-script recipe.

title: defmulti with custom :hierarchy for isolated derive tables
code:
;; (defmulti name dispatch-fn :hierarchy H) uses H (from make-hierarchy)
;; for ancestor-walk dispatch instead of the shared global-hierarchy.
;; This keeps tests and libraries from leaking `derive` calls into a
;; shared table.

(def events-h (make-hierarchy))
(derive events-h 'order/new-member-order 'order/any)
(derive events-h 'order/returning-order  'order/any)
(derive events-h 'order/any              'event/any)
(derive events-h 'user/signup            'event/any)

(defmulti handle-event
  (lambda (evt) (cdr (assq 'type evt)))
  :hierarchy events-h)

(defmethod handle-event 'order/new-member-order (e)
  (str "welcome-discount for order " (cdr (assq 'id e))))
(defmethod handle-event 'order/any (e)
  (str "logged order " (cdr (assq 'id e))))
(defmethod handle-event 'event/any (e)
  (str "generic audit for " (cdr (assq 'type e))))

;; When both 'order/any and 'event/any match a value like
;; 'order/new-member-order (both are ancestors), dispatch raises
;; an ambiguity error unless you express preference:
(prefer-method handle-event 'order/any 'event/any)

(displayln (handle-event '((type . order/new-member-order) (id . 1001))))
(displayln (handle-event '((type . order/returning-order)  (id . 1002))))
(displayln (handle-event '((type . user/signup)            (id . 42))))
notes: Without :hierarchy, defmulti uses global-hierarchy — fine for demos, but `derive` mutations leak across tests. Pass an isolated hierarchy when you want clean state. Ambiguity (multiple ancestor methods apply) raises unless resolved with `prefer-method`. Implementation note: `:hierarchy` is an auxiliary keyword exported from (std multi); importing the library brings it into scope for the syntax-rules literal match.

title: s-defn: speced define that works in --script programs
code:
;; s-instrument rewrites a name's top-level binding via
;; set-top-level-value!. In a --script program, earlier top-level
;; forms may have already captured the unwrapped closure — so the
;; instrumented wrapper never fires. s-defn solves this by baking
;; arg/ret validation into the function body at expansion time.

(s-def ::isbn   (s-and string? (s-pred (lambda (s) (> (string-length s) 10)))))
(s-def ::qty    (s-and integer? (s-pred positive?)))
(s-def ::order  (s-cat ':isbn '::isbn ':qty '::qty))
(s-def ::result (s-cat ':tag symbol? ':isbn '::isbn ':qty '::qty))

;; Full form: :args and :ret (order doesn't matter).
(s-defn place-order (isbn qty)
  :args '::order
  :ret  '::result
  (list 'order isbn qty))

(displayln (place-order "978-0553560732" 2))  ;; → (order 978... 2)

(guard (_ [else (displayln "rejected")])
  (place-order "short" -1))

;; Only :args — validate inputs, pass-through return.
(s-defn upcase-str (s)
  :args (s-cat ':s string?)
  (string-upcase s))

;; Only :ret — validate return, pass-through args.
(s-defn as-sym (s) :ret symbol? (string->symbol s))

;; No specs — degenerates to (def ...).
(s-defn plain-add (a b) (+ a b))

;; s-check-fn still works because s-defn registers an s-fdef.
(displayln (s-check-fn 'place-order place-order '("978-0553560732" 7)))
notes: Prefer s-defn over (def ...) + s-instrument for code that runs via --script. Keywords :args and :ret may appear in any order; both, either, or neither may be omitted. s-defn registers an s-fdef so s-check-fn and introspection tools see the specs. Validation error messages include the spec explain-str for the offending value.

title: Clojure test.check-flavor generative testing (quick-check, defspec)
code:
(import (jerboa prelude))
(import (std test check))

;; (std test check) re-exports everything under both flavors:
;;   gen:integer / gen:string / for-all / check-property         (jerboa-style)
;;   gen/int     / gen/string / prop/for-all / quick-check       (Clojure-style)
;; Pick whichever you prefer; they share one engine + rose-tree shrinker.

;; --- write properties with prop/for-all (note: gen/int is a *value*, gen/list-of is a fn) ---

(define commutative-+
  (prop/for-all ([x gen/int] [y gen/int])
    (= (+ x y) (+ y x))))

(define reverse-involutive
  (prop/for-all ([xs (gen/list-of gen/int)])
    (equal? xs (reverse (reverse xs)))))

;; --- run a property: returns a hashtable result map ---

(define result (quick-check 100 commutative-+))

(hash-ref result 'result)        ;; => #t on success, #f on failure
(hash-ref result 'pass?)         ;; => same as 'result
(hash-ref result 'num-tests)     ;; => 100

;; On failure, you also get:
;;   'fail          — first failing input list
;;   'failing-size  — index where failure happened
;;   'shrunk        — alist with 'smallest (minimal failing input) and 'depth

;; --- defspec gives you a thunk you can call from mat / your test runner ---

(defspec spec/+commutes 50
  (prop/for-all ([x gen/int] [y gen/int])
    (= (+ x y) (+ y x))))

(spec/+commutes)   ;; => result map

;; --- built-in generators ---
;;   gen/boolean   gen/byte   gen/int   gen/nat   gen/pos-int   gen/neg-int
;;   gen/large-integer
;;   gen/char   gen/char-alphanumeric
;;   gen/string   gen/string-alphanumeric   gen/string-ascii
;;   gen/keyword   gen/symbol   gen/uuid
;;   gen/list-of   gen/vector-of   gen/hash-set-of   gen/hash-map-of

;; --- combinators ---
;;   gen/return v
;;   gen/fmap f g            ;; map output through f
;;   gen/bind g (lambda (v) g')  ;; monadic bind
;;   gen/choose lo hi        ;; uniform integer
;;   gen/elements coll       ;; pick one of
;;   gen/one-of (list g ...) ;; pick generator, then sample
;;   gen/such-that pred g    ;; reject + retry
;;   gen/frequency '((w . g) ...)
;;   gen/tuple g ...
;;   gen/sized (lambda (n) g)
;;   gen/no-shrink g

;; --- inspect what a generator produces ---
(gen/sample gen/int 10)          ;; => list of 10 ints
(gen/sample gen/string-alphanumeric 5)
notes:
Round 13 (2026-04-27) added a Clojure test.check-style API on top of the existing rose-tree shrinker. Both flavors coexist: the colon names (gen:integer, for-all, check-property) are the original jerboa API; the slash names (gen/int, prop/for-all, quick-check, defspec) match clojure.test.check.

quick-check returns a regular hashtable (jerboa hash-ref works directly). On failure, the `shrunk` entry contains the smallest input the shrinker could find — the rose tree halves integers toward 0, removes elements from lists/vectors, and lifts shrinks through gen/fmap and gen/bind.

Note: gen/uuid produces v4-shaped hex strings but is *not* RFC-4122 cryptographically random; it's fine for property testing.

Seed reproducibility is not yet wired in — Chez `random` is process-global. Pass `max-size:` as a third arg to quick-check if you want to cap the size growth (default 200).

title: Clojure-style defprotocol / extend-type / extend-protocol
code:
(import (jerboa prelude))
(import (std clojure protocol))

;; ----------------------------------------------------------------
;; Define a protocol — a set of methods that dispatch on the type
;; of their first argument.  Each method becomes a regular procedure.
;; ----------------------------------------------------------------

(defprotocol Greetable
  (greet [this])
  (farewell [this name]))

;; ----------------------------------------------------------------
;; Extend the protocol for a type using a one-argument predicate.
;; ----------------------------------------------------------------

(extend-type String? Greetable
  (greet [this] (string-append "Hello, " this))
  (farewell [this name] (string-append "Bye " name " from " this)))

(extend-type Number? Greetable
  (greet [this] (str "Hello, number " this))
  (farewell [this name] (str "Bye " name " from " this)))

(greet "world")        ;; => "Hello, world"
(greet 42)             ;; => "Hello, number 42"
(farewell "Earth" "Alice")  ;; => "Bye Alice from Earth"

;; ----------------------------------------------------------------
;; Multi-type extend-protocol form (predicates must be identifiers).
;; ----------------------------------------------------------------

(defprotocol Sizeable
  (sz [this]))

(extend-protocol Sizeable
  String? (sz [this] (string-length this))
  Vector? (sz [this] (vector-length this))
  List?   (sz [this] (length this))
  Hash?   (sz [this] (hashtable-size this)))

(sz "hello")           ;; => 5
(sz (vector 1 2 3))    ;; => 3
(sz '(a b c d))        ;; => 4

;; ----------------------------------------------------------------
;; Extend a defrecord — use the auto-generated `<name>?` predicate.
;; ----------------------------------------------------------------

(defrecord person (name age))

(extend-type person? Greetable
  (greet [this] (str "Hi, I'm " (person-name this)))
  (farewell [this who] (str who ", from " (person-name this))))

(greet (make-person "Alice" 30))   ;; => "Hi, I'm Alice"

;; ----------------------------------------------------------------
;; Introspection
;; ----------------------------------------------------------------

(satisfies? Greetable "world")   ;; => #t
(satisfies? Greetable #t)        ;; => #f (no Boolean? extension)
(extends? Greetable Number?)     ;; => #t
(protocol-name Greetable)        ;; => Greetable
(protocol-methods Greetable)     ;; => (greet farewell)

;; ----------------------------------------------------------------
;; Built-in predicate aliases (Clojure-style names)
;; ----------------------------------------------------------------
;;   Number?  String?  Vector?  List?  Pair?  Null?
;;   Keyword? Symbol?  Char?    Boolean? Hash? Bytevector?
;;
;; Use these when you want extend-protocol to read like Clojure;
;; you can also pass any one-argument predicate (e.g. (extend-type
;; (lambda (x) (and (number? x) (positive? x))) Greetable ...)
;; via extend-type — multi-type extend-protocol requires identifiers).
notes:
Round 14 (2026-04-27) closes the last common Clojure-idiomatic gap: a general-purpose value-dispatch protocol mechanism. Note the existing (std actor protocol) defprotocol is actor-specific (ask/tell/reply); this one is for ordinary polymorphism, modeled on clojure.core.

Dispatch is a linear walk over (predicate . method-alist) entries. First matching predicate wins. Re-extending a type replaces the previous registration for that predicate (idempotent).

Limitation: extend-protocol's multi-type form requires predicate identifiers (not lambda expressions) so the macro can distinguish them from method forms. For expression-valued predicates, use separate extend-type calls.

defmulti remains the better choice when dispatch needs a custom selector (multi-arg, value-based, hierarchical). defprotocol is just sugar for "dispatch on the type of arg 1".

title: Sleeping inside fork-thread without silently killing the thread
code:
;; In a Jerboa file that imports (jerboa prelude) (or :jerboa/core, which
;; re-exports prelude bindings), make-time is SHADOWED by a date-style
;; constructor. So this looks correct but raises silently:
;;
;;   (fork-thread
;;     (lambda ()
;;       (let loop ()
;;         (sleep (make-time 'time-duration 0 5))   ;; <- raises
;;         ...)))
;;
;; The exception kills the thread before the first iteration. No
;; output. Any logic that depended on the timer never fires (watchdogs
;; that should close hung connections; escape-key watchers; retry loops).
;;
;; Two correct alternatives:

;; 1. thread-sleep! from :std/misc/thread (or via :jerboa/core).
;;    Accepts fractional seconds.
(import :std/misc/thread)
(fork-thread
  (lambda ()
    (guard (e [#t (log-error logger "watchdog-died" `((err . ,(err->string e))))])
      (let loop ()
        (thread-sleep! 5)        ;; 5 seconds
        ;; (thread-sleep! 0.020) ;; 20 ms
        (when (still-running?) (loop))))))

;; 2. sleep-ms from (jerboa prelude) — only available when you import
;;    (jerboa prelude) directly, not via :jerboa/core.
;; (sleep-ms 5000)  ;; 5000 ms
notes: CRITICAL PATTERN: ALWAYS wrap a fork-thread body in (guard (e [#t ...]) ...) when it contains a long-running loop you cannot afford to lose. A silent exception in any iteration -- not just sleep -- will terminate the thread without logging. We hit this in jerboa-code with a stream watchdog that should have closed hung TLS connections after 90s; the watchdog died on its first sleep call and the connection sat in CLOSE_WAIT for 500+ seconds. The shadowed make-time was the trigger but the unguarded loop was what made it a silent hang instead of a visible error.

title: char-ready? returns #t at EOF — always check (eof-object? c) when polling
code:
;; WRONG -- pegs a CPU at 100% the moment the input port hits EOF.
;; (char-ready? port) reports #t at EOF in Chez, and (read-char port)
;; then returns the eof-object without consuming anything, so the
;; loop never sleeps and never terminates.
(def (drain-stdin!)
  (let loop ()
    (when (char-ready? (current-input-port))
      (read-char (current-input-port))   ;; returns eof-object at EOF
      (loop))))                          ;; loops forever, no sleep

;; RIGHT -- bind the result and break out on EOF.
(def (drain-stdin!)
  (guard (e [#t (void)])
    (let loop ()
      (when (char-ready? (current-input-port))
        (let ((c (read-char (current-input-port))))
          (unless (eof-object? c)
            (loop)))))))

;; Same trap in a watcher thread that polls for a key in cbreak mode:
(def (start-escape-watcher!)
  (spawn
    (lambda ()
      (guard (e [#t (void)])
        (let loop ()
          (if (char-ready? (current-input-port))
            (let ((c (read-char (current-input-port))))
              (cond
                ((eof-object? c) (void))   ;; stdin closed -- exit thread
                ((and (char? c) (= (char->integer c) 27))
                 (signal-abort!))
                (else (loop))))
            (begin (thread-sleep! 0.020) (loop))))))))
notes:
Triggered in jcode when stdin was piped (echo "hi" | jcode --no-tui): the streaming response printed fine, then drain-stdin! and the escape-watcher thread both spun at 100% CPU because (char-ready? port) returns #t at EOF.

Interactive TTY use never showed the bug because read-char blocks instead of returning eof on a TTY with no pending input; piped/redirected stdin hits EOF and the polling loops never break out.

Rule: any loop that uses (char-ready? port) as its sentinel MUST also bind the (read-char port) result and check (eof-object? c). The else branch (no char ready) needs an explicit (thread-sleep! ...) before recursing too, otherwise a busy port still pegs a CPU.

Fix landed as commit dd22a54 in jerboa-code: src/jcode/ui/cli.ss drain-stdin! + start-escape-watcher!.

title: Use __collect_safe for any blocking foreign-procedure (recv, accept, lock, sleep)
code:
;; A plain (foreign-procedure ...) holds the Chez thread-context (TC)
;; mutex for the ENTIRE duration of the C call. While the C side blocks
;; on a syscall (recv, poll, accept, lock acquire, long compute), no
;; other Scheme thread runs -- not the GC, not a watchdog, not a
;; mailbox drain. Declaring it __collect_safe releases TC so other
;; Scheme threads can be scheduled while the C call is in flight.

;; WRONG -- silently freezes every other thread for the duration of
;; the recv(). If the peer goes silent, the streaming watchdog spawned
;; by fork-thread never gets CPU and can never fire its timeout.
(def jerboa-tls-read
  (foreign-procedure "jerboa_tls_read"
    (unsigned-64 u8* unsigned-64) int))

;; RIGHT -- TC is released for the duration of the recv(), so a
;; watchdog or sibling thread can run and (e.g.) close the fd to
;; unblock us.
(def jcode-tls-read
  (foreign-procedure __collect_safe "jerboa_tls_read"
    (unsigned-64 u8* unsigned-64) int))

;; Pattern: when the upstream binding (here in :std/net/tls-rustls)
;; is NOT __collect_safe and you cannot edit it, declare a private
;; foreign-procedure to the SAME C symbol with __collect_safe and
;; use that locally. The symbol is already loaded once the upstream
;; library has been imported.
(import (std net tls-rustls))   ;; loads libjerboa_native.{so,dylib}

(def jcode-tls-read
  (foreign-procedure __collect_safe "jerboa_tls_read"
    (unsigned-64 u8* unsigned-64) int))

(def (rustls-read-safe conn buf len)
  (jcode-tls-read conn buf len))
notes:
Caused a real, hard-to-diagnose hang in jcode: deepseek's SSE stream stalled mid-round-2, the main thread blocked in tls-read-line -> rustls-read -> jerboa_tls_read and held TC indefinitely. The 90s read-timeout watchdog spawned via fork-thread never got CPU, so it never fired. Symptoms looked like "fork-thread silently fails" or "thread-sleep! never returns", but the real cause was the blocking FFI not yielding TC.

The Jerboa stdlib (as of 2026-04) declares networking FFI bindings without __collect_safe -- (std net tls-rustls), (std net tcp), (std os signal), etc. Don't trust them to be TC-safe; inspect the source. If you can't modify upstream, shadow the binding locally with __collect_safe.

Rule of thumb: any C call that may block on I/O, a lock, or a long computation MUST be declared __collect_safe. Pure CPU-fast calls (strlen, getpid, math) don't need it.

Live example: src/jcode/provider/provider.ss declares jcode-tls-read locally and uses it in place of rustls-read; src/jcode/ui/tui-ffi.ss does the same for the termbox poll. Fix landed in jerboa-code at commit 9160039.</notes>
<parameter name="related">["spawn-thread", "tui-event-loop-mailbox-drain"]
